entry.title: Custom Logic using Key User Extensibility in SAP S/4 HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
Introduction

In On Stack extensibility, Enhancements can be created using Custom Logic App in Key User Extensibility.

Key user extensibility also know as In App extensibility, extensions can be added with key user tools.

 

Key User Extensibility / In App Extensibility- Custom Logic

Custom Logic is one of the Key User Extensibility Tools                        

In the Custom logic App, 

  • Custom logic can be added
  • Easy exploration of extension points, key user documentation
  • Syntax check, syntax highlighting, code completion
  • Draft, published, testing included
  • ABAP Language Version for Key Users
  • Enhancements can be created using Custom Logi App

Business Accelerator Hub

SAP Business Accelerator Hub provides a section for On Stack extensibility (key user extensibility and developer extensibility), to know list of SAP CDS views, APIs, Business Add-ins etc. are available with the release status.

Navigate to Business Accelerator Hub to find BAdis

On Stack Extensibility -> Business Add-ins

Search for required BAdi.

This BAdi is related to check purchase order before saving to create it.

Here it shows BAdi Definition and Enhancement spot.

BAdi can be used in both Key User and Developer Extensibility as both states of BAdi is in released state.

Methods and its Parameters

 

Steps to Create Custom Logic

Navigate to Custom logic App

Custom Logic App

Choose Create button to create Custom Logic.

Choose Required Business Content

 

List of available BAdi Definitions for the business context.

Choose required BAdi Definition.

Its Documentation.

Choose Step 2 to add Implementation Description and ID.

Implementation ID automatically appears based on Description and it can also be changed.

Choose Review

Choose Create

Click on Publish button.

Its published.

Click on Open Code Editor to write the logic.

Importing Parameters.

Changing Parameters.

Sampe Code -> Show Sample Code

Keyboard Shortcuts -> three dot menu -> Show Keyboard Shortcuts

Write the required logic.

IF purchaseorder-supplier EQ '0000100005'.
APPEND VALUE #( messagetype = 'E' messagevariable1 = |Choose another Supplier| ) TO messages.
ENDIF.

 Navigate to Create Purchase order (ME21N)

Provide the required details and check.

Error is appeared as per logic written.

This implementation class can be opened in the eclipse, and break point can be added in the logic, and it triggers when creating purchase order in the Create Purchase Order (ME21N).

Key-user objects can be displayed and debugged but cannot be edited with ABAP development tools.

 

Conclusion                     

In Key User Extensibility,

Custom logic can be added and Enhancements can be created using Custom Logic App.

Own implementation can be created and those can be deleted.

Implementation can be published to test, production systems

Code can be compared with older code versions.

 

References

  • Custom Logic | SAP Help Portal
  • Custom Logic Tracing | SAP Help Portal
  • Extensibility | SAP Help Portal
  • Key User Extensibility | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: Re: Week 2 – Building AI Agents with CrewAI and Generative AI Hub
modification en masse des demandes d’achat: MEMASSRQ
entry.content:
Présentation de la transaction :

Il s’agit d’une transaction standard utilisée pour la modification en masse des demandes d’achat (Purchase Requisitions).

MEMASSRQ permet de modifier simultanément plusieurs demandes d’achat en une seule opération, au lieu de les modifier individuellement via ME52N.

Les champs concernés :

D'après l'interface système, les champs les plus couramment modifiés en masse sont :

 

 

 

 

Procédure de lancement :

1er Méthode : Modification par Saisie Écran

Étape 1

  1. Lancez MEMASSRQ.
  2. Dans l'onglet Zones, sélectionnez le champ à modifier (ex: EBAN-FLIEF pour le fournisseur attitré).
  3. Cliquez sur Exécuter (F8).

 

Étape 2 : Filtrage des demandes d’achat (DA)

  1.  

    Renseignez les numéros de DA concernés.
  2. Cliquez sur Exécuter.

 

 

Etape 3 :  Application et Sauvegarde

  1. Saisie : Dans la ligne "Nouvelles valeurs" en haut, saisissez la donnée cible (ex: code fournisseur 22715).
  2. Sélection : Sélectionnez les lignes à modifier dans le tableau (utilisez le bouton "Tout sélectionner")
  3. Transfert : Cliquez sur l'icône "Effectuer modification en masse"

 

Sauvegarde: Cliquez sur la Disquette (Sauvegarder).

Cette action est indispensable pour valider l’enregistrement en base de données.

Résultat immédiat: La colonne en surbrillance (orange) se met à jour et affiche la nouvelle valeur (ex. : 22715 au lieu de 4134).

Compte rendu

  • Une fois l'enregistrement terminé, SAP affiche automatiquement un Log de traitement
  • Icône Vert : modification effectuée avec succès.
  • Icône jaune : modification effectuée avec avertissement.
  • Icône rouge : modification  échouée.

Traçabilité des modifications

Vérification via Table EBAN

Vérification des Protocoles via la transaction SLG1

 
  • Causes fréquentes : DA verrouillée par un autre utilisateur, budget insuffisant, ou incohérence de données (ex: fournisseur non créé pour cette division), poste supprimé
  • Historique : Toutes les modifications effectuées via MEMASSRQ sont consultables dans chaque DA (Transaction ME53N, onglet Statut ou menu Environnement > Modifications du document)

entry.title: Advanced API Testing and Simulation with OSV : Practical Examples
entry.content:
BACKGROUND

This article is a follow-up to two previous blog posts:

  • API Testing with Tricentis Test Automation for SAP (TTA) & SAP Enterprise Continuous Testing  where I introduced the basics of API testing.
  • Orchestrated Service Virtualization (OSV) with SAP Enterprise Continuous Testing by Tricentis (ECT)  where I detailed the configuration and general operation of the API simulation service.

If you haven't read these articles yet, I highly recommend checking them out before proceeding with this one.

In this article, I will present three advanced examples of API testing and simulation using OSV:

  1. How to test a complete API workflow composed of multiple requests
  2. How to test multiple API values using TestSheets
  3. How to define a generic response for unknown requests

Focus 1: Testing a complete API workflow (1 Request / N Responses)

This section outlines the process for testing an API that provides different responses to identical requests.

In this example, an API is called with 3 parameters (surname, name, age), leading to the creation of a record in the backend. The API confirms the successful addition and returns the ID of the new record. If the same request is submitted again, the API responds with a message indicating that the record already exists.

To test this type of process, the API will be scanned twice to capture the responses. The TestSteps will then be assembled as follows:

The process can then be visualized as follows by clicking on the "Sequence Flow Diagram" tab:

It's worth noting that this interaction can also be easily converted into an OSV scenario.

Once deployed, the scenario can be accessed in the OSV Monitor.

Focus 2: Testing different API Values using TestSheets (N Requests / N Responses)

This section explains the procedure for testing multiple API requests with varying values. In the example below, the API is called to retrieve the stock price in different currencies. The request includes a single parameter representing a currency (GBP, EUR, or USD). The API responds with a numeric value.

To create a more versatile test, value ranges will be defined for each currency, and the expected results will be summarized in the following TestSheet:

Next, the test script is converted into a template, and the TestSheet is mapped to the TestCase.

 Finally, the TestCase can be instantiated to generate the test scripts:

You can also create an OSV service based on the instantiated template if desired.

Focus 3: Defining a generic response for unknown requests

In this final section, the procedure for configuring OSV to handle specific responses for requests that differ from the predefined ones will be outlined. In the example below, an API simulated via OSV returns the stock price in two currencies (USD and EUR).

 The OSV scenarios are structured as follows:

If a request is made with an undefined currency, such as CHF, the system will return:

404 Not Found 
No scenario found that matches the incoming message.

For example, if the requirement is that undefined currencies (i.e., any currency other than USD or EUR) should return the value "999," this can be represented as follows:

 To achieve this behavior, the following scenario will be created and deployed:

When testing the OSV service, it is observed that while the behavior is correct for all currencies other than USD and EUR, an error occurs for USD and EUR. The error message is:

Found multiple matches with the same weight, aborting this match completely!
Scenario: Stock Price Request EUR; Step: 1; Weight: 0; Rules ->
    Value: 'EUR' Path: currency
Scenario: Stock Price Request OTHER CURRENCIES; Step: 1; Weight: 0; Rules ->
    Value: '*' Path: currency

This issue arises because both EUR and USD match multiple scenarios with the same weight. Specifically, "EUR" matches the scenario Stock Price Request EUR with the value "EUR" and also matches the Stock Price Request OTHER CURRENCIES with the value "*".

To ensure that the Stock Price Request OTHER CURRENCIES scenario is used as the default response only when other scenarios are not applicable, a Virtualization parameter called WEIGHT will be added.

To implement this, go to the OSV module "Request" and add the WEIGHT parameter as a Module Attribute.

Next, set the Weight value to -1 in your Stock Price Request OTHER CURRENCIES scenario and redeploy it.

Your service is now functioning as desired!

WHAT'S NEXT!

This article presents advanced examples of API testing and simulation with OSV, including testing complete API workflows, handling varying values with TestSheets, and defining generic responses for unknown requests. These insights complete our exploration of API and OSV integration with Tricentis on-prem tools (TTA and ECT).

Follow my profile for updates on future posts, and feel free to ask any questions in the comments below!

entry.title: Sous traitance SAP MM
entry.content:
Étapes du processus de sous-traitance :

  1. Création d’une fiche article de type matières premières– MM01
  2. Création d’un article de type produit fini- MM01
  3. Création d’un fournisseur– BP
  4. Creation d’une FIA-ME11 de type sous-traitant. L’article dans ME11 doit etre le produit fini. Le prix est le prix de service rendu par le sous-traitant
  5. Création d’un bon de commande type de poste « S» ME21N . L’article dans le poste est le produit fini 6. Vérifier l'état de stock - MMBE
  6. Créer EM pour réapprovisionner le stock avec des matières premières -MIGO (Code Mvt 561)
  7. Vérifier l'état de stock - MMBE
  8. Transfert/Sortie des matières premières au sous-traitant -ME2O (code Mvt 541)

10.Vérifier l'état de stock - MMBE

11.Réception du produit fini MIGO avec reference du BC (Code Mvt 101)

  1. Réception de la facture– MIRO

13.Afficher facture – FB03

Création d’un article type matières premières MM01: 759

 

Création d’un article type matières premières MM01: 747

 

 

d’un fournisseur– BP : 1000521 - ABCD

 

d’une FIA

 

Prix de service

d’un Bon de commande. Type de poste “S”

 

Type de poste S Prix de service, et n’ont pas de l’article

Prix injecté depuis la FIA

 

Exploser les nomenclatures (BOM) dans le BC

 

Cliquer sur composants

Exploser les nomenclatures (BOM) dans le BC

 

 
 

 


Créer des EM pour réapprovisionner les stocks (Code Mvt 561)

 

Stock de sous-traitance- ME2O

Utiliser vos propres articles et fournisseurs.

Verifier l’etat du stock – MMBE (stock mis a diposition du fournisseur)

Créer sortie de marchandises

Réception du produit fini avec réfeéence BC- MIGO (Code Mvt 101)

Réception de la facture– MIRO

Afficher la facture -FB03

entry.title: Check out the Expert Guided Implementation (EGI) sessions for May 2026
entry.content:
Check out the Expert Guided Implementation (EGI) sessions for May 2026.  EGI’s are instructor led sessions.  The sessions below are designed to facilitate effective adoption and utilization of the SAP Signavio solutions.  EGI's are part of your SAP Enterprise Support or SAP Enterprise Support, Cloud Edition contract, which means there’s no additional cost for participating in these sessions.

Important:  The links in this blog connect to the SAP Learning Hub, edition for SAP Enterprise Support, which requires a one-time registration. 

 Sign up | Walkthrough video 

May 2026 sessions are outlined in this blog with brief descriptions. 

                                                     ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

✔️SAP Signavio Adoption Starter:  Value Accelerators, Process Explorer, and Process Navigator [English].  Register Here.  This course helps you to adopt and consume the capability and usability of the SAP Signavio Process Explorer to enable business users to run in-depth process analysis. Also get an insight on country- and deployment-specific SAP solution value accelerators using Process Navigator, different deployment options, countries, and localization requirements.

Goals:  

  • Get SAP Signavio Process Explorer is set up and ready to use.
  • Understand how Value Accelerators can speed up your transformation journey.
  • Understand SAP Signavio Process Navigator.
  • Understand how to Navigate through different value accelerators​​.

                                               ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

✔️SAP Signavio Adoption Starter: Process Intelligence [English, Spanish, and Portugues]. Register Here.  This offering helps you to adopt and consume the capability and usability of the SAP Signavio Process Intelligence to enable business users to run in-depth process analysis. SAP Experts will guide you through Connect, Upload, and Analysis of the data by creating a process Data Management pipeline and overall feature demonstration. 

Goals: 

  • Set up SAP Signavio Process Intelligence for advanced process analysis.
  • Understand process mining concepts and their business impact.
  • Learn to connect, upload, and analyze data through a structured pipeline.
  • Leverage accelerators to maximize insights and efficiency.

                                           ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

✔️SAP Signavio Adoption Starter:  Process Insights for IT [English]. Register Here.  In this course you will learn how to connect your SAP System to SAP Signavio Process Insights and collect relevant data to get ready for quick process insights.

Goals: 

  • Learn how to seamlessly connect your SAP system to SAP Signavio Process Insights.
  • Gain confidence in administering and managing the solution effectively.
  • Discover how to use analytics and improvement tools to drive business optimization.

                                        ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

✔️SAP Signavio Adoption Starter:  Process Insights for Business – Sales [Portuguese and Spanish].  Register Here.  This offering helps you to adopt and consume the capability and usability of the SAP Signavio Process Insights to enable business users to run in-depth process analysis. Learn How to analyze your Lead-to-Cash process with SAP Signavio Process Insights.

Goals: 

  • Analyze Lead-to-Cash process data for better decision-making.
  • Implement recommendations to optimize sales workflows.
  • Prepare for S/4HANA migration with data-driven insights.

                                     ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

✔️SAP Signavio Adoption Starter:  Process Manager and Collaboration Hub [English and Chinese]. Register Here.  This course is designed to facilitate the effective adoption and utilization of SAP Signavio Process Manager and Collaboration Hub for process management and modeling.

 Goals:

                                    ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

entry.title: Re: Cloud ALM User
entry.content:
HI  @vitor_wolke1 

I've been discussing this also with the SAP CALM implementation experts. The problem is that the recommended option for role assignment for CALM is to use the ALM App: https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-alm/setup-administration/role-assignment

This is not aligned with most of customers want and requiere manual manteinance of assignments in ALM app. As discussed with them, the option with BTP role collections is still possible as included in the documentation above but i still not sure why ALM tram developed a different app to maintain permissions and recommends it to be used.

We're using let's say option 2 for all BTP services so we ended up configuring ALM with the same. In our scenario we go one step forward and map some custom BTP Role collections for ALM to Microsoft Entra groups and we manage all the assignmets from there. That's what most Security teams want so they can keep control of all of the identites and their permissions from a central place (the IDP, i.e Entra). When a user is locked/deleted in Entra he/she won't be able to access ALM anymore. If ALM permissions are removed from Entra, they user won't be able to access ALM features.

The idea of synching users and authorizations using IAS/IPS makes a lot of sense but someone from Security would still prefer to manage their users centrally from Entra (or it's IDP like Google, Okta, etc). In medium size companies the complexity of managing transformations in IAS for 20/30 systems to provision and deprovision users is HUGE. SAP Cloud Identity services is a great tool and offers some reporting and trobleshooting capabilities, but you need to understand that orchestrating synchs for many systems with many different attributes is complex and CIS does not have some "orchestration layer" to handle everything alltogether. we just have IPS transformations and jobs. As I said, it's great tool but is no IDM tool tahta allows full orchestration and central view of the connected systems and Identities

Thanks

Diego

entry.title: Unpredictable Amount in Lease in contract
entry.content:
Dear expert,

This question is create for solution discussion for Unpredictable Amount in Lease-in Contract.

Business Background:

We have several vendor sending recurring invoice for the lease contract. However, due to inforecast business impact, the invoice from vendor could be different with the invoice generated from Leased-in module (1T6).

Any protentional solution for this. How could we deal with the difference which the total amount of AP should also considered in the asset we transferred (IFRS ledger).


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Reduce SOFFCONT1 table size in database
entry.content: Dear, can we have some reference concerining Pre-check : Verificatin of active links via SRGBTBREL + Ensure no business objects still reference SOFFCONT1 This is often missed and causes broken attachments post cleanup. ?

entry.title: Re: Unpredictable Amount in Lease in contract
entry.content:
Thank you for your question!

Ideally, the invoices issued by the vendor in a lease-in scenario should match the contractual obligations outlined in the contract. The conditions should be maintained with the correct contractual periodic amounts as the conditions themselves form the basis for valuation.

However, if that is for some reason not feasible - using statistical conditions in this scenario may be an avenue worth exploring. Statistical conditions, while still forming the base of valuation entries do not generate vendor cash-flow entries and are not cash-flow relevant.

Periodic posting directly from the contract will not be possible for statistical conditions, but then manual booking of invoices with whatever amount necessary can be explored.

I recommend reviewing the scope item test script for scope item 1T6 available on the SAP Process Navigator, where you can learn more about the process of assigning conditions to a lease contract as well as other best practices related to Contract and Lease Management in a lease-in scenario.

entry.title: Re: Recursive BOM: CK739 and CK740 error in CK11N for Rec...
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

We were able to run a standard cost estimate for a recursive BOM. However, it only works when an additional component (other than the recursive component) (or) an activity cost (or) an overhead cost is included. *This Material is not having special procurement key in its master*.

Based on the behavior, it seems the logic may be that a recursive input component alone cannot generate the output product. In other words, the same material (e.g., 512345) with a lower quantity cannot, by itself, produce the same material with a higher quantity.

That said, I have not found any official documentation confirming this logic. If anyone can validate or clarify this behavior, it would be greatly appreciated.

 

#Recursive BOM #CK11N #CK740 #CK739, Standard Cost estimate

entry.title: How to enable PKCE in Trusted Applications?
entry.content:
When creating Authorization Code Grant in Trusted Applications to enable SAP CPQ Plugin in Visual Studio, there is one option to enable PKCE.

How to configure it?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: How to enable PKCE in Trusted Applications?
entry.content:
Please follow these steps to use PKCE with SAP CPQ Plugin in Visual Studio:

  1. Set up Authorization Code Grant in Trusted Applications
  2. Enable the PKCE
  3. On Visual Studio, download CPQ Plugin and open it
  4. Configuration of environment is needed providing:
    1. URL
    2. Client Secret
    3. Client Id
  5. At the end, you will have the toggle to enable PKCE, switch this on
  6. Save it and try to login
  7. This will popup and at accepting, will send you to browser to accept and open the environment at Visual Studio.

This allows you to use PKCE, giving more security for the environment.

entry.title: Re: Monday morning data problems - lessons learned
entry.content:
Hi Martin,

Thanks for sharing and contributing to the community!

My story speaks to self-affliction 🙂 One Friday evening I had one last step to finish a project. Rushing to get home, I moved a secondary index to production on one of SAP's largest tables without informing our basis/dba team. I got a "nice call" that weekend after all our jobs canceled due to no disk storage space. Monday morning everyone was cleaning up the fallout. Moving forward, we worked towards better communication between teams along with coordinated go-live planning. Definitely a growth experience for me as a data professional.

entry.title: Re: Story Report Filter Error
entry.content: hi all, we are experiencing the same issues. Unfortunately I have not been able to find any improvement request. On bad days it happens multiple times, next day the table is fine.

entry.title: Re: Somos SAP Community Curitiba – Luiz Souza
entry.content:
Quero começar agradecendo vc, @LuizSouza_cwb , junto a @SimoneAzevedopelo convite e principalmente pelo trabalho consistente na construção da SAP Community Curitiba. Iniciativas como a de vcs mostram, como comunidade é um dos pilares mais estratégicos para o desenvolvimento profissional, ampliando networking qualificado, fomentando aprendizado contínuo e criando espaços seguros para troca real de experiências.

Acredito muito que comunidades fortes aceleram carreiras, aproximam pessoas e constroem ecossistemas sustentáveis. Inclusive, boa parte da minha própria transição de Oracle JDE para o ecossistema SAP foi motivada exatamente por essa natureza colaborativa, pelo acesso ao conhecimento compartilhado e pelo engajamento genuíno entre profissionais. Por isso, fico realmente feliz em fazer parte da SAP Community Curitiba e contribuir ativamente com esse movimento.

Quem sou eu na SAP Community Curitiba?

Prazer! Sou Dênis Junior, SAP Security & SAP GRC Engineer, atuando desde 2019 atuando na convergência entre segurança, governança, risco, compliance e arquitetura técnica SAP.  Atualmente, trabalho em ambientes corporativos globais de alta criticidade, liderando design e governança de segurança para SAP RISE, S/4HANA, BTP e Cloud, sempre com foco em security by design e alinhamento a frameworks como SOX, ISO 27001 e ITGC.

Dentro da SAP Community Curitiba, contribuo trazendo uma visão estratégica e técnica de SAP Security, conectando arquitetura, governança e inovação. Meu objetivo é ajudar a elevar o nível de maturidade das discussões, mostrando que segurança bem desenhada é um facilitador da inovação, e não um obstáculo.

O que mais me motiva a continuar fazendo parte da SAP Community Curitiba?

O que mais me motiva é o poder do coletivo. A comunidade cria um ambiente onde conhecimento é compartilhado de forma prática, onde profissionais se apoiam, crescem juntos e discutem desafios reais, desde Clean Core, SAP Security BTP, SAP Security RISE, até temas críticos como Identity, Access Management, governança e compliance.

Além disso, estar próximo de pessoas que vivem SAP no dia a dia, trocando experiências reais e não apenas conceitos teóricos, me mantém constantemente desafiado e alinhado com a evolução do ecossistema.

Como fazer parte da comunidade impactou minha jornada profissional ou pessoal?

A comunidade teve um impacto direto e profundo na minha trajetória. Profissionalmente, ampliou meu networking e abriu espaço para trocas com profissionais altamente qualificados, no Brasil e no exterior.

Pessoalmente, fortaleceu minhas habilidades de comunicação, liderança e pensamento estratégico, além da confiança para contribuir ativamente em discussões técnicas e arquiteturais complexas.

Como me vejo na comunidade daqui a 2 anos?

Daqui a dois anos, me vejo contribuindo com conteúdos, debates e iniciativas focadas em segurança, governança e arquitetura SAP moderna. Quero ajudar a amadurecer a discussão sobre segurança desde o design, especialmente em cenários de Cloud, IA, RISE, BTP e Clean Core, apoiando a comunidade a construir soluções cada vez mais seguras, escaláveis e alinhadas ao futuro.

Pegando o gancho do Luiz, deixo como frase final:
construir comunidade é construir futuro.  A SAP Community prova que quando pessoas se conectam, compartilham e aprendem juntas, o crescimento deixa de ser individual e passa a ser exponencial.

Conecte-se comigo
Dênis Junior | LinkedIn

entry.title: SAP Serial Number Stock Inconsistency: Fix MB52 vs IQ09 Mismatch
entry.content:
In SAP, inconsistencies between material stock (MB52) and serial number stock (IQ09) can occur due to process gaps, manual errors, or incomplete transactions. These mismatches can lead to serious reporting and operational issues if not corrected promptly.

This blog explains a practical and effective approach to resolve such inconsistencies using the Physical Inventory process.

📊Problem Scenario

A mismatch exists between stock quantities:

MaterialLocationStock in MB52Serial number Stock in IQ09Observation
40114RM016 PC5-1 (missing serial)

⚠️Why This Happens

Common reasons include:

  • Incorrect goods movement postings
  • Serial number not updated properly
  • Manual adjustments without synchronization
  • Incomplete WM or IM processes

🛠️ Solution Approach: Using Physical Inventory

Instead of direct corrections, SAP best practice is to use physical inventory to align both stock and serial numbers.

Step 1: Check Current MB52 Stock  (Transaction: MMBE/MB52)

Step 2: Check Current Serial Numbers (Transaction: IQ09)

Note: Total MB52 stock is 6 PC but in IQ09 only 5 serial numbers are available (missing 1 serial number / excess mmbe stock)

Step 3: Create Physical Inventory Document (Transaction: MI01)

Step 4: Enter Count for physical Inventory Document (Transaction: MI04)

Note: Once you enter Quantity, system pops up asking for serial numbers.

Step 5: Review Serial Number (Transaction: MI04)

Step 6 : Change Serial Numbers (if required) (Transaction: MI04)

Select the line item for which serial number to be changed and click on change serial numbers.

Step 7: Post Differences (Transaction: MI07)

Review and save the document to post differences.

Step 8: Check MMBE Stock post differences (Transaction: MB52/MMBE)

Step 9: Check Updated Serial Numbers (Transaction: IQ09)

Note: Total stock in MB52/MMBE matched with serial numbers in IQ09.

 

If you found this guide helpful, don't miss any other SAP MM WM blogs covering real-world automation, configurations tips, and best practices for procurement, inventory management, and warehouse management.

Click to view other SAP MM WM Blogs - SAP Community

 

Thanks,

Leelamohan Mannem

entry.title: First Half 2026 Release: What’s New for SAP SuccessFactors Performance & Goals
entry.content:
Modern performance management must do more than document outcomes. It needs to continuously align work to strategy, surface risks early, and enable fair, confident talent decisions at scale. In the 1H 2026 release, SAP SuccessFactors Performance & Goals takes a significant step forward with agentic AI innovations and automation designed to reduce administrative effort, improve goal quality, and strengthen decision consistency across the organization.

If you want to go deeper into how these innovations can be adopted in your organization, watch this short Performance & Goals expert video. Some of our experts talk through real customer challenges, how these agents work together, and where to start. We’re excited to help you build a more aligned, agile, and high‑performing workforce with SAP SuccessFactors Performance & Goals.

Let’s take a closer look at what’s new.

Reinventing Goal Management with Agentic AI

Many organizations struggle with goals that are unclear, misaligned, or quickly outdated. That creates unnecessary friction for employees and managers. With this release, SAP introduces two new agentic AI capabilities that transform how goals are created, maintained, and acted on throughout the performance cycle.

Goal Creation Agent

Creating high‑quality goals is often more time‑consuming than it should be, particularly when employees and managers must interpret role expectations, business priorities, and goal plan requirements on their own. With the Goal Creation Agent, we are introducing an agentic AI capability that takes the complexity out of that process. Using a conversational experience, the agent helps employees generate meaningful, actionable goals that are automatically aligned to their role, organizational objectives, and configured goal plan rules. Instead of starting from a blank page, employees receive relevant, well‑structured goals in minutes, giving them immediate clarity on priorities and expectations. This dramatically reduces manual effort while improving goal quality and strategic alignment from the very start of the performance cycle.

Goal Creation Agent on the homepage

Goal Progress Agent

Even well‑written goals can quickly lose relevance as business needs change, yet many organizations only discover misalignment during performance reviews. And sometimes it’s already too late to course‑correct. The Goal Progress Agent addresses this challenge by continuously monitoring goal health throughout the performance cycle. It automatically identifies goals that are outdated, misaligned, or at risk and proactively prompts timely refinements, ensuring work stays focused on what matters most right now. By delivering continuous quality assurance instead of point‑in‑time checks, the Goal Progress Agent helps employees avoid end‑of‑cycle surprises and enables managers to stay ahead of issues without added administrative burden. The result is a more agile, scalable approach to goal management that unlocks hours of productivity and keeps performance aligned with evolving business priorities.

GoalProgress Agent on the homepage

Stronger Fairness and Consistency with Powerful New Business Rules

As organizations adopt more continuous and skills‑driven performance models, ensuring fairness, consistency, and credibility across performance evaluations becomes increasingly complex. To address this, the first half 2026 release introduces a new set of new powerful business rules in performance forms that automate key decision logic and reduce manual oversight. These rules are designed to strengthen the integrity of the performance process by applying the same standards to every employee, regardless of manager or geography.

With these enhancements, organizations can automatically detect conflicting rating logic within performance forms, such as unusually high goal ratings combined with low competency ratings, helping surface potential inconsistencies before evaluations are finalized. New rules also allow performance improvement plans to be launched automatically when defined conditions are met, enabling earlier intervention and more structured support for employees who need it. For high performers, dynamic rules can reveal next‑level competencies tied to future roles, encouraging forward‑looking development conversations and helping employees assess themselves against what comes next. In addition, organizations can now automate the calculation of overall performance ratings based on different combinations of goal and competency outcomes, ensuring evaluations are balanced, standardized, and applied consistently.

Together, these new business rules help organizations reduce bias, improve data quality, and strengthen confidence in performance outcomes. By embedding fairness, accountability, and automation directly into the process, SAP SuccessFactors Performance & Goals enables managers to focus less on administrative decisions and more on meaningful coaching and development conversations.

Business rules in performance forms

A More Agile, Confident Performance Experience

Together, these innovations create a more responsive and scalable performance management experience — where goals stay aligned, risks surface earlier, and performance outcomes are easier to trust and act on.

With SAP SuccessFactors Performance & Goals in 1H 2026, organizations can:

  • Reduce administrative effort for managers
  • Keep work continuously aligned to business needs
  • Improve fairness and confidence in performance decisions
  • Enable employees to grow with clarity and purpose

You can discover more 1H 2026 innovations across the suite by checking out the release highlights brochure, overview video featuring Bianka Woelke, and much more on the Product Release & Road Map Information page.

Learn more about SAP SuccessFactors Performance & Goals.

entry.title: SAP Fieldglass May 2026 Release: Explore the Latest Features with the What’s New Viewer
entry.content:
Available on the SAP Help Portal, the What’s New Viewer provides an interactive, customizable way to explore all new features and updates included in the upcoming release. Its intuitive filters and layout make it easy to browse high-level summaries, read detailed explanations, and view supporting visuals designed to help you quickly understand the impact of each change.

Filtering the What’s New Viewer

Use the Automatically On enablement filter to highlight the features and/or changes that are available to you on day 1, provided you have access to the impacted area. No action is required on your part to get the feature or the change.

To view these items:

  1. Under Release, select 2026.05.0.0 to display May 2026 items only
  2. Under Enablement, select Automatically On

Export your filtered results

The filtered results can be exported as a PDF, which includes additional item details and screenshots where available. PDFs are limited to a maximum of 200 entries. To export, select PDF in the top right of the page, then click Download PDF.

Need help or have questions?

Read the Exploring What's New in SAP Fieldglass guide for more information on how to use the What’s New Viewer.

If you have any questions or concerns about the information in the What’s New Viewer or the May 2026 release, submit a case through SAP for Me or the SAP Fieldglass Help Center.

Stay up to date

Visit our SAP Community page and view our May 2026 Release Resources page to keep track of all the latest SAP Fieldglass release information.

entry.title: Agentic Memory Unpacked: Families, Types, Architectures, and Lifecycle
entry.content:
In my previous article, I gave an overview of agentic memory — what it is, when to use it, and how it relates to learning. This article delves deeper into memory families, content types, organizational architectures, and the memory lifecycle. After reading it, you will be equipped with all the knowledge and vocabulary necessary to start your own memory experiments and step up as a competent discussion partner on the matter.
 
This article draws heavily on Memory in the Age of AI Agents: A survey, augmented by my own thoughts and examples. Memory in the Age of AI Agents is a great (and very long) read that paints a comprehensive picture of agentic memory research. This post can't substitute the full paper, but I hope it provides a practical starting point — and I invite you to adopt its taxonomies and vocabulary.
 

Memory Families: Where and how do agents store memories?

 

Vanilla LLMs are stateless across separate interactions. This observation serves as the basis for all discussions of memory. However, it is important to note that this does not mean that LLM cannot store knowledge. In fact, everything an LLM "knows" is encoded in its model weights. It is thus logical to consider continuous adaptation of model weights during runtime to store memories and insights on user and environment interactions. Because this strategy encodes memory information in model parameters, it is also known as parametric memory.
 
Another form of memory that lives close to the core of the LLM is latent memory. Latent memory is formed not in the model weights, but in its hidden states and caches that can be snapshotted, persisted, and loaded back into the next session. Both parametric and latent memory are advanced concepts that require deeper model understanding; future posts will cover them.

Finally, token-level memory refers to memory created and persisted in various forms outside the LLM, which is then injected into the context window of a new session as tokens.
 

 Token-level, Parametric, and Latent Memory:  Description and Analogy
Memory FamilyDescriptionHuman Analogy
Parametric memoryKnowledge encoded directly in the model's weights through training or fine-tuning. For example, a model fine-tuned on company-specific terminology and workflowsLearned and deeply internalized skills like riding a bike — you don't consult a manual, you know
Latent memoryInternal representations, such as hidden states or caches that can be persisted and restored. For example, a snapshotted KV cache from a prior session or compressed hidden state checkpoints.The "mental context" you hold while working on a problem — lose focus, and it's gone
Token-level memory
Explicit, discrete memory stored externally and injected as tokens into the LLM context window. Examples include conversation logs, user preference files, shared documents, and knowledge snippets retrieved from dedicated storage structures.
A notebook you carry to note down and develop thoughts and observations



Due to the nature of these memory families, they come with some inherent pros and cons:


Token-level, Parametric, and Latent Memory:  Comparison 
PropertyToken-level MemoryParametric & Latent Memory
AuditabilityAuditable, potentially human-readable, symbolicNon-auditable, non-human-readable
ExplainabilityHigh — memories can be inspected and understood directlyLow — individual updates are only meaningful within the context of all other weights
EfficiencyRetrieval overhead and increased latency during inferencePrincipally higher efficiency; no retrieval step needed
ReliabilityAdditional RAG link in the chain makes end-to-end pipeline potentially less reliableKnowledge is embedded in the model itself; similar reliability to world facts in LLM
Measurability
Full scan and inspection of stored knowledge is possibleCan only be measured by probing; no meaningful full scan
Natural fit
Tasks that need auditing, high-level strategies (type 2 thinking - deliberate, analytical)Fuzzy tasks like image recognition and control; tasks that escape explicit rule sets (type 1 thinking — fast, intuitive)

 

The remainder of this article focuses exclusively on the most intuitive and actively discussed memory family: token-level memory.
 

Memory Contents: What do we store?

 

My previous article discussed basic considerations regarding agentic memory use cases, but knowing when to use agentic memory is one thing; knowing what to store is another.

To delineate which types of content can be stored in agentic memory, I find it helpful to look at memory models. Drawing on a background of psychology and neuroscience, Endel Tulving proposed a memory model originally intended to describe human memory. This model is now often referenced in AI. Tulving's model distinguishes "semantic", "procedural", and "episodic memory". A slightly different but overlapping model is put forward in Memory in the Age of AI Agents. In it, "factual memory", "experiential memory", and "working memory" are distinguished.

 
Memory Models: Types, Subtypes, Description, and Examples
Lifespan & Memory TypeTulving EquivalentSubtypeDescriptionExample
Long-term:
Factual		SemanticUser factualFacts and preferences about the user."The user prefers concise answers."
  Environment factualFacts about the environment, domain, or world the agent operates in."The production database uses PostgreSQL 15."
Long-term:
Experiential		Procedural + EpisodicCase-basedMinimally processed records of historical episodes. Retain specific context and outcomes.A full interaction log of a debugging session that resolved a timeout error.
  Strategy-basedDistilled, transferable reasoning patterns, workflows, and high-level insights from past trajectories. Decouple decision-making from specific contexts."When facing erratic tests, first check for shared state between test cases before investigating timing issues."
  Skills-basedExecutable procedural capacities like atomic code snippets or other artifacts that operationalize abstract strategies.A reusable shell script for rotating log files, derived from repeated manual operations.
Short-term: Working——Capacity-limited, dynamically controlled collection of information for active context management during a single task or session. Erased after the session ends. The current conversation history, to-do lists, or scratchpad notes.
 

Notice that no strict boundaries exist between these types: memory architectures may or may not physically separate them, and one type can eventually be derived from the other. For example, information stored in working and episodic memory may be abstracted and condensed to form long-term semantic or procedural memories.
 

Information Architectures: How are memories organized?

 

Independent of the type of individual memories, they may be related; like a cluster of user preferences for text formatting (semantic), a set of events with a temporal history (episodic), or a bunch of code snippets for interacting with an external data source (procedural). The recent popularity of knowledge graphs is a testament to the importance of such relations in many agentic applications, prompting us to consider how memories may be connected.

The following are prototypic forms of organizations that an agentic memory corpus may have. In my opinion, it is helpful to distinguish and name them, because they are the perfect bridge between what we expect the memory to do and how we implement it structurally:
  • Flat Memory (1D): No explicit inter-unit topology. Memories are accumulated as sequences or bags of units (e.g., text snippets, trajectories, chunks). They may reference one another implicitly, e.g., "as known from a recent conversation".
  • Planar Memory (2D): A structured but single-layer organization within one plane: units are related by a graph, tree, table, and so on, with no cross-layer relations. The structure is explicit, but not layered (e.g., a single knowledge graph).
  • Hierarchical Memory (3D): Structured across multiple layers with inter-layer links, forming a volumetric or stratified memory. (e.g., multiple knowledge graphs at different levels of abstraction for layered retrieval.)

Just a few days ago, Andrej Karpathy shared his view on the importance of relations among agentic memories under the concept of an "LLM Wiki". Irrespective of whether we call this dynamic knowledge base a wiki or a memory corpus, the importance of cross-referencing in many practical applications is clear, as is the goal of creating and maintaining "a persistent, compounding artifact" of connected information.
 

Memory Lifecycle: How does it all come together? - A cradle-to-grave memory perspective

 

Bearing potential content and organizational structures in mind greatly helps articulate the purpose of an agentic memory system and begin drafting an implementation architecture. There is, however, another perspective that influences design: the memory lifecycle — from creation through alteration to eventual deletion.

The following are the three main lifecycle phases of agentic memory:
  • Formation – Capturing novel insights from interactions and experiences.
  • Retrieval – Intelligently surfacing relevant information to enhance the agent's context and decision-making for future tasks.
  • Evolution – Refining, synthesizing, and connecting stored knowledge through reflection.


Memory Formation "How to extract the memory (from traces)?"

Agentic memory forms from observable artifacts — agent logs and traces — but memories are typically more refined than raw logs. Forming them involves processing steps like the following:
  • Semantic Summarization is a lossy compression mechanism, distilling the gist of lengthy interaction logs. It does not interpret or refine the contained information other than by reducing noise and compressing it as is. Semantic summarization reduces context length, allowing more core insights to be loaded into a single LLM context window upon retrieval. Also, this form of summarization may help LLM focus on important parts of a log.
  • Knowledge Distillation aims to extract higher-order reusable knowledge from interaction trajectories and artifacts. This may involve relating a current episode to prior learning signals and records to spot a pattern carrying reusable knowledge, e.g., "this is the 10th time I am being asked to refactor a result to include docstrings, I will assume that the user always wants docstrings until further notice."
  • Structured Construction builds on top of memory units produced by semantic summarization and knowledge distillation. Structured construction is used to relate pieces of information to one another, i.e., to build a higher-order organization from flat memory units.

These processes often co-occur: processing a single interaction log might involve summarizing it, distilling a skill from recurring patterns, and linking that skill to related context descriptions for future retrieval.
 

Memory Retrieval "How to utilize memory?"

Agentic memory ultimately has to serve its purpose during runtime. At some point, an agent will be asked to provide help with a task that benefits from information contained in the memory corpus. Memory retrieval is fundamentally a RAG problem — the same metrics (accuracy, speed, cost) and techniques apply, just drawing from a memory-specific corpus. It is thus not a focus of this article. However, retrieval signals (e.g., frequency) can feed back into the evolution of memory, as you will see below.
 

Memory Evolution "How to refine the memory?"

Forming memories from agent traces provides the memory base with new insights. However, these insights may contradict, add to, or relate to prior memories. This is why long-running agentic memory solutions will inevitably need to offer ways to evolve memories over time, possibly even outside of active user sessions:
 

Memory Evolution Mechanisms: Purpose and Key Challenges
MechanismPurposeKey Challenge
ConsolidationMerge new and existing memories into generalized insights, ensuring cumulative learningDetermining the right granularity for matching and merging
UpdatingResolve conflicts between new and existing memories to maintain accuracy and relevanceStability-plasticity dilemma: when to overwrite vs. treat as noise
ForgettingRemove outdated or redundant information to maintain focus and efficiencyOverly aggressive pruning risks erasing rare but essential knowledge
 
 
In principle, humans can supply feedback or support throughout any of these lifecycle phases. E.g., humans can inspect the memory database and mark memories as outdated, update memories, or merge memories. This behavior is already implemented in solutions like ChatGPT's memory. In addition to manual and LLM work, deterministic rules can be used to evolve the memory base. E.g., memory forgetting can be modeled using deterministic measures such as time since creation or retrieval frequency.


What's Next?

 

This article, alongside its predecessor, serves as an introduction to agentic memory. Upcoming articles on the matter will focus on deep dives into topics like latent memory. With them, we are moving closer to the cutting edge of research and industry.
 
 
# CTO Research and Innovation Munich
@ladenaw

entry.title: SPROXY shows “Service outdated” only for newly created Integration Suite Service Interfaces
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

we are currently testing ABAP proxy generation with SAP Integration Suite.

We observed the following behavior in transaction SPROXY:

  • Migrated PI/PO service interfaces can be activated and do not show this issue
  • New service interfaces created directly in SAP Integration Suite can also be used to generate ABAP proxies
  • Runtime communication works: the ABAP proxy can receive messages from Integration Suite
  • However, in SPROXY these newly created interfaces remain in status “Service outdated”
  • Regeneration and activation of the proxy do not remove the status

 

So the issue seems to occur only for service interfaces modeled natively in Integration Suite, not for interfaces migrated from PI/PO.

Questions:

  1. Is this a known limitation or bug of ABAP proxy generation with newly created Integration Suite service interfaces?
  2. Can the “Service outdated” status be ignored if runtime communication is already working?
  3. Has anyone managed to get rid of this status for newly created interfaces?

Any experience would be greatly appreciated.

KR

Stefan

entry.title: Business Objects Enterprise 4.3 update from SP04 patch 8 to SP5 patch 4 failed !
entry.content:
Hello guys
I'm having big problems trying to upgrade an SAP Business Objects Enterprise 4.3 system, from SP4 patch 8 to SP05 patch 4.
First of all, I report the data of the system:
- SAP Business Objects Enterprise 4.3 SP04 Patch 08 single node.
- The web application server is Apache Tomcat 9.0.91.
- The CMS database is a locally installed SQL Anywhere 17 (17.11.7458).
- Everything is hosted by a Windows Server 2022 version 21H2 VM (OS Build 20348.4773).
- As CPU, the VM has 8 sockets and 16 virtual processors.
- The RAM amounts to 128GB.
The upgrade is necessary to close some security flaws found in the current version of the product (SAP notes 3654236, 3695912, 3697256).
I tried, for the first time, this upgrade, in mid-March and the activity ended "encountering errors".

The errors reported in the logs (ErrorsAndWarnings.log files) were all related to the impossibility of copying or moving files, for example:

Errors/Warnings found during the installation are as follows

17:45:48.198 Error: Could not move temp dest folder to dest folder.
17:45:48.198 Error: Temp dest folder: D:\SAP BusinessObjects\InstallData\tmp\monitoring.sdk.java-4.0-core-64
17:45:48.198 Error: Dest folder: D:\SAP BusinessObjects\InstallData\InstallCache\monitoring.sdk.java-4.0-core-64\14.3.5.5591\
17:45:48.198 Error: Class name: class Poco::FileAccessDeniedException
17:45:48.202 Error: Display text: Access to file denied: \\?\D:\SAP BusinessObjects\InstallData\tmp\monitoring.sdk.java-4.0-core-64
17:45:48.202 Error: Could not delete temp dest folder: D:\SAP BusinessObjects\InstallData\tmp\monitoring.sdk.java-4.0-core-64\
17:45:48.202 Error: Class name: class Poco::FileAccessDeniedException
17:45:48.202 Error: Display text: Access to file denied: \\?\D:\SAP BusinessObjects\InstallData\tmp\monitoring.sdk.java-4.0-core-64\actions\strings\ar.uitext.xml

(There are about 124 log lines with errors of this type).

I opened a SAP case about that and, the responsible for the problem, was identified in the antivirus and EDR software Microsoft Defender and CrowdStrike.
Then, I tried the upgrade again, after uninstalling CrowdStike and having some folders put in the Windows Defender’s antivirus exclusion list (the folder where SAP Business Objects is installed, and the folder where the update packages are stored).

As always, I followed all the directions in the SAP note 1952120 before I started.
I carried out the task, executing the upgrade exe file "as administrator" with a user that is member of the Administrators group of the machine, which is also the same user with which the SIA service is set.
Before starting, there was 153GB of free space on the drive where SBOP is installed and 66.2GB of free space on the system C: drive.
I made a first attempt to upgrade, to version 4.3 SP05 patch 4.
After a few minutes from the beginning of the procedure, I saw the file:
ErrorsAndWarnings.log quickly become 3KB large.
The activity then continued and, an hour and twelve minutes later, the upgrade ended with the message:
“SAP Business Objects BI platform 4.3 SP5 Patch 4 has encountered errors during the install process”.

The ErrorsAndWarnings.log log file contained virtually the same type of errors as the first upgrade attempt.

At that point, thinking that there might be something wrong with the upgrade packages, I reverted the virtual machine to the state before the upgrade (so system to version 4.3 SP04 patch 8 fully functional) and I tried a new upgrade to version 4.3 SP05 patch 5 (upgrade package released a few days ago).
Unfortunately, although the file ErrorsAndWarnings.log had far fewer errors, the upgrade was still finished, in an hour and fourteen minutes, always with the message:
“SAP Business Objects BI platform 4.3 SP5 Patch 5 has encountered errors during the install process”.

The ErrorsAndWarnings.log log file has the following content:

Errors/Warnings found during the installation are as follows

22:22:54.539 Error: Could not move temp dest folder to dest folder.
22:22:54.539 Error: Temp dest folder: D:\SAP BusinessObjects\InstallData\tmp\crystalreports.cpp.exporting.u2frdef-4.0-fr-64
22:22:54.539 Error: Dest folder: D:\SAP BusinessObjects\InstallData\InstallCache\crystalreports.cpp.exporting.u2frdef-4.0-fr-64\14.3.5.5657\
22:22:54.539 Error: Class name: class Poco::FileAccessDeniedException
22:22:54.539 Error: Display text: Access to file denied: \\?\D:\SAP BusinessObjects\InstallData\tmp\crystalreports.cpp.exporting.u2frdef-4.0-fr-64

However, I would like to point out that, although the upgrade ended with errors, the system seemed to be working correctly: all the processing servers were up & running and even the test of opening a report and its refresh did not give me problems.

I preferred, however, to revert the virtual machine and deliver the working system to the customer, albeit to the old version.

Do you have any idea what it may depend on?
I opened a new case to SAP support, but, in the meantime maybe somebody may help me.

Thank you very much.
Andrea

entry.title: Re: Practice System is full right now, but no worries......
entry.content:
Dear @verorach_97,

SAP does not publish a fixed wait time for that message. The only official timing SAP gives is that, after successful enrollment, some systems are available immediately and others may take up to 90 minutes to prepare but that is a different situation from a system being full

More details below:

https://learning.sap.com/helpcenter/learninghub-subscription/practice-systems

What to do:

Check whether you already have two active practice-system enrollments because SAP allows only two simultaneous course enrollments. If the issue continues raise a support case and select Practice Systems as the service category SAP states that this support team is available 24/5

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Reuse a DTV (Data Transition Validation) Project in a New BTC Cycle
entry.content:
Overview

When you create a new cycle in the Manage Cycles app, the system automatically creates a new DTV project. In this situation, you may want to reuse the settings from an existing project.

Prerequisites

You created a new cycle in the Manage Cycles app and set the Run Data Transition Validation (DTV) Tool option to Yes.

Procedure

  1. In transaction DTV, export the project that has the settings you want to reuse.

  2. The system automatically creates a new DTV project when you create the cycle. In transaction DTV, locate this project and copy the project ID. This ID will be required in step 4.

    Note: The project ID is the same as the cycle ID that you can view in the Manage Cycles app.

  3. Change the project ID of the newly created project to a temporary name.

  4. Restart transaction DTV and import the project you exported in step 1, but specify the project ID you copied in step 2. This creates a new project with the project ID of your new cycle and that contains the configuration settings you want to reuse.

  5. (Optional) Delete the project you renamed in step 3.

entry.title: Re: Table names and string translation
entry.content:
Do You mean the second option with the stored procedure "TmSp_GetDefaultElementsforCR"?

No, you don't need any subreports. You only need to add the stored procedure "TmSp_GetDefaultElementsforCR" to the layout without linking it.

This procedure has two parameters: DocKey@ and ObjectId@. Therefore, the result of this procedure will be a single row with many columns (one column per text entry). The row contains the translations in the language of the document.

Here is the corresponding SAP note, where all of this is described:

https://me.sap.com/notes/2785541

This option (with the stored procedure "TmSp_GetDefaultElementsforCR") should be selected if the client requires translations in multiple languages. However, if only German and English are sufficient, I would recommend choosing the first option with variables for each text entry for performance reasons.

entry.title: 001 The "IDEA" in the Room
entry.content:
Two-thirds of business leaders say they don't fully trust their data. For organizations trying to deploy AI into critical workflows, that's not a statistic — it's a structural problem. The people closest to it — the data leaders, the architects, the CDOs navigating it daily — knew this long before the analysts did. And yet, until recently, nobody had built an institution to solve it.

This is the story of how one got started. And how you can get involved.

---

With an operational mind and background in accounting, I'm wired to see problems in terms of processes — where the money flows, where the logic breaks, where the gap between the model and the reality quietly widens until someone has to explain it to a board.

I grew up in Vancouver, built a career at SAP, and oddly I've spent more than a healthy amount of time thinking about institutions. The ones that exist because a problem was real enough, and persistent enough, that the people living inside it eventually decided to stop waiting for someone else to fix it.

That instinct had been with me for a long time. It took a dinner on a rainy Tuesday night in Vancouver to finally do something about it.

---

The first iteration surfaced around 2017. I was working inside SAP's New Ventures and Technology group — a genuinely progressive initiative that gave people like me the room to think commercially, take real risks, and build something with teeth. Along with David Brockington, I co-founded ADAZA by SAP there. Innovative work, interesting problems, and a front-row seat to the Canadian startup ecosystem as I began to build relationships with local accelerators.

What I saw was a talent community that was genuinely impressive in capability but often narrowly focused in ambition. A lot of energy going toward consumer apps and niche digital tools, while industries with massive, expensive, structurally complex problems sat a few hundred kilometers away. Forestry. Mining. Energy. Canada's economic backbone. The kind of industries where a well-aimed technology intervention doesn't just improve a conversion rate, it changes the economics of an entire operation.

The concept I kept returning to was a bridge. A proper institute — independent, practitioner-led — connecting the people who knew how to build with the industries that had something worth building for. Not a conference. Not a vendor program. A place where ideas met problems, and both sides left smarter.

It didn't happen. The timing wasn't right, the path wasn't clear, and I wasn't ready. But the shape of the idea stayed with me.

---

Fast forward to 2024. I'd spent the better part of six years leading operations for SAP's Business Technology Platform (BTP) marketing group, before becoming Chief of Staff to our CMO. Consequential work, but I'd been orchestrating someone else's programs for long enough. I wanted to own something again. Build something. Create direct impact, with a clear line from effort to outcome.

The vision of a think tank returned. This time, it had found the right problem.

I'd been watching the same conversation play out, over and over, across the data and AI landscape. Intelligent, experienced leaders — the people responsible for some of the most complex data environments on the planet — all carrying the same quiet frustration. Years of investment. Sophisticated platforms. Governance committees. And still, when it came time to actually trust the data underneath a consequential decision, there was a pause. A hesitation. A quiet but persistent gap between what the architecture was supposed to deliver and what the business actually experienced.

The numbers make the gap concrete. Nearly half of enterprises report active struggles with data fragmentation. Poor data quality costs the average organization $12.9 million per year — and that's the average. In a large, complex enterprise — a global bank, say, with decades of system accumulation, regulatory overlays, and acquired platforms that were never properly integrated — the figure is almost certainly higher, and largely untracked.

Meanwhile, AI had arrived at the party and was asking uncomfortable questions. Not about the technology — the models were impressive, the use cases were compelling. The problem was what sat underneath them. As one executive put it at a roundtable we later ran in Sydney: "98 percent of the world's data is not inside the large foundation models. It's locked in enterprises — in the systems." That's not a technology gap. That's a foundation problem.

Everyone was experimenting with AI. Copilots, generative tools, workflow automation — pilots were running in almost every organization we spoke with. But converting those experiments into scaled enterprise capability was proving elusive, particularly in regulated sectors where core business workflows depend on sensitive proprietary data, compliance requirements, and high-stakes decisions. Deploying AI into those environments requires something most organizations hadn't finished building: a data foundation they could actually trust.

The barrier wasn't the model. It was the meeting that had to happen before the model could be used. As another data leader at the same roundtable in Sydney put it rather directly: "The painful part for me is the governance around that… most of my time is going through all of the committees." Architecture review. Third-party risk. Responsible data governance bodies. Necessary, all of it — and collectively consuming the bandwidth of some of the sharpest people in the organization.

Scaling AI is less a technical challenge than a governance one. That line became a thesis.

---

What I couldn't find, in 2024, was a credible and independent space where senior leaders could sit down, be genuinely honest about this problem, and work through it together — across industries, across geographies, without a vendor agenda in the room. Analyst firms produce reports. Conferences produce panels. Vendors produce whitepapers. Nobody had built the institution.

Why does nobody own this?

That question had been sitting in the back of my mind for months when Dan Yu, our CMO for Data & Analytics, came to town.

Dan was in Vancouver for team meetings during what was, diplomatically, a period of organizational change. We carved out a Tuesday evening for dinner at Vij's, a local favourite just down the street. The kind of place where the food is good enough that you stop performing and just talk.

I didn't walk in with a deck. I walked in with a direction. A non-profit research organization dedicated to the advancement of data architecture and enterprise AI. Independent. Practitioner-led. Built on the insight that the problem wasn't technical — it was organizational, cultural, and structural. And that the only way to move the needle was to get the right people in a room, outside the commercial context, with enough trust to be honest about where things actually stood.

Dan had been part of establishing the TBM Council in a previous chapter of his career — a serious, respected organization that had done exactly this kind of work for technology business management. He understood the model. He understood what it took to build something that lasted. And he could see, as clearly as I could, that nothing like it existed for the data architecture conversation.

He didn't need much convincing.

We finished dinner. I walked home. And within a couple of weeks, I was writing up the plans.

---

There's a line from IDEA's first research brief that I keep coming back to: "The quality of your decisions will never exceed the quality of your data." Simple. Almost obvious. And yet most organizations are operating with a trust deficit they haven't fully priced. Companies that treat data as a strategic asset — not an IT problem — are 2.3 times more likely to grow revenue using AI. They make decisions 33% faster. They're not smarter. They've just fixed the foundation.

That was the gap IDEA was built to address. Not another vendor platform. Not another industry conference. An independent forum where the people responsible for enterprise data could work through the real problems together — and produce something the field could actually use.

What followed that dinner was the beginning of an experiment: could we build a genuinely independent space for the people responsible for the world's most complex data environments to solve this problem together? The roundtables, the research, the partnerships, the arguments about what the institution should and shouldn't be — that's what this is about.

---

*Next: Getting SAP to say yes — which, it turned out, was the easy part.*

---

**About IDEA**

IDEA — the Interchange for Data and Enterprise AI — is a programme of research, community building, and peer learning, delivered in partnership with the Open Data Institute (ODI). The ODI brings 14 years of experience stewarding independent, multi-stakeholder research across public and private sectors. Together, we're building a space where enterprise data leaders can cut through the noise, share what's actually working, and help shape a vendor-agnostic blueprint for AI-ready data infrastructure.

The programme is actively seeking involvement from data leaders, industry partners, and academic experts. If you work at the intersection of data, governance, and enterprise AI — and you have something real to contribute — we'd like to hear from you.

--> Find out more and get involved

Thanks in advance.

entry.title: Re: SAP Agentic AI in Practice: Concepts, Architecture, and Your First LangGraph Agent (Part 1 of 6)
entry.content:
@chetan_099 Thank you for your questions.

On the SDK package name — you are correct. generative-ai-hub-sdk is now deprecated and the replacement is pip install sap-ai-sdk-gen (SAP Cloud SDK for AI - Python). The good news is that class names are unchanged so all code in the series continues to work — only the installation command needs updating. I will revise the articles accordingly. Good catch.

On where to run the code and the LeanIX SAP AI agent hub question:

For the development environment, SAP BAS is the recommended option for interactive development and notebook-based exploration as covered in Part 2 of the Generative AI Hub series. For production, the agents in this series are designed to deploy on BTP Cloud Foundry as Flask or FastAPI applications.

On LeanIX — the SAP AI agent hub there is a governance and discovery layer, not an orchestration runtime. It helps organizations inventory and govern their AI agents across the enterprise. It doesn't replace LangChain or LangGraph — those still handle the reasoning, tool calling, and execution logic at runtime. Think of LeanIX as the control plane for agent governance and BTP as the runtime where agents actually execute. The two are complementary rather than competing, and your LangChain/LangGraph orchestration code is unaffected by it.

entry.title: How to traslate KTD documents for SAP BTP ABAP Environment?
entry.content:
Introduction

When you create a KTD document in SAP BTP ABAP Environment you will find out that there is no support for the translation of these documents.  

In this blog post I will describe a workaround that can be used until a standard solution will be provided by SAP.

Workaround

I managed to translate a KTD document “manually.”

  1. I logged in with German and created a KTD document.
  2. Then I exported the KTD document into a Git repository.
  3. Then I created a new entry in the XML for the language key “E” , chose a new UUID as the key, and entered the new text Base64 encoded (!). 
  4. Finally I imported the content of the Github repository again, thereby creating a translated version of the KTD document. 

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<abapGit version="v1.0.0" serializer="LCL_OBJECT_SKTD" serializer_version="v1.0.0">
 <asx:abap xmlns:asx="http://www.sap.com/abapxml" version="1.0">
  <asx:values>
   <KTD_OBJECT>
    <NAME>ZZ10R_CART</NAME>
    <TYPE>GENERAL_TEXT</TYPE>
    <SOURCE_CONTENT_TYPE>02</SOURCE_CONTENT_TYPE>
    <REF_OBJ_TYPE>DDLS</REF_OBJ_TYPE>
    <REF_OBJ_NAME>ZZ10R_CART</REF_OBJ_NAME>
    <ABAP_LANGUAGE_VERSION>X</ABAP_LANGUAGE_VERSION>
    <CONTENT>
     <item>
      <NAME>ZZ10R_CART</NAME>
      <LANGU>D</LANGU>
      <COMPONENT_UUID>1rDFSNW0H9Cq1O2Gk1jBBg==</COMPONENT_UUID>
      <SOURCE_CONTENT>RG9rdSBpbiBEZXV0c2NoCgphbmQgbm93IGFkZCBzb21ldGhpbmcgbG9nZ2VkIG9uIGluIEVuZ2xp
c2g=</SOURCE_CONTENT>
     </item>
     <item>
      <NAME>ZZ10R_CART</NAME>
      <LANGU>E</LANGU>
      <COMPONENT_UUID>9u8rknFsH+Cq1DFMgPUzaA==</COMPONENT_UUID>
      <SOURCE_CONTENT>IyBub3cgb3JpZ25hbCBlbmdsaXNo</SOURCE_CONTENT>
     </item>
    </CONTENT>
   </KTD_OBJECT>
  </asx:values>
 </asx:abap>
</abapGit>

As a result, I see different texts when I am logged in with English or German.

Known issues

• If you edit a KTD document with ADT while being logged in in another language, you will “see” the version created in the other language. There is no error message.
• In such a case, you can even edit the KTD document and the changes are visible for both login languages.
• Only if, as described above, you export the KTD document via the “Github” detour and create a version in another language, will you have two texts.
