entry.title: From Copilot to Colleague: How SAP Is Shaping the Next Era of AI Driven Procurement
entry.content:
From Copilot to Colleague: How SAP Is Shaping the Next Era of AI‑Driven Procurement 🤖

Artificial intelligence has crossed a critical threshold in enterprise software. What began as experimentation with chat interfaces has rapidly evolved into a strategic conversation about how work itself is performed. In a recent discussion with Ryan Skorupski, Senior Director of AI/ML at SAP Ariba, the conversation explored how AI is moving from assistive tooling to embedded intelligence—and what that shift means for procurement leaders navigating increasing complexity, data volume, and speed expectations. 🚀

AI Didn’t Arrive Overnight—But the Moment Has Arrived 🌱

While generative AI may feel new, the technologies behind it—machine learning, language modeling, and predictive analytics—have been advancing in academia and research environments for more than a decade. What changed was accessibility. When large language models became available to a broader audience, leaders experienced firsthand how AI could reason across information, synthesize insights, and support decision‑making at scale.

For procurement, this marked a turning point. AI was no longer limited to narrow automation use cases; it became capable of understanding context, language, and intent. As Ryan noted, this moment forced organizations to rethink not just what they automate, but how procurement work should be designed in an AI‑first world. 💡

Beyond Chatbots: The Rise of the Procurement Copilot ✈️

Much of today’s AI adoption centers on copilots—context‑aware assistants embedded directly into enterprise applications. In SAP Ariba, copilots are designed to work alongside users, helping them navigate sourcing events, analyze data, and interact with complex workflows using natural language.

Unlike traditional chatbots, copilots are grounded in business context. They understand procurement data, permissions, and workflows, allowing them to assist with real tasks—not just answer questions. This represents a significant step forward in usability and productivity, particularly for teams balancing speed, compliance, and accuracy. 🔍

The Next Leap: Agentic AI in Procurement 🤝

While copilots enhance productivity, Ryan highlighted that the next major evolution is AI agents—systems capable of taking action, not just providing guidance. An AI agent can assemble an RFP, analyze historical sourcing events, recommend negotiation strategies, and prepare supplier communications with minimal human intervention.

Importantly, the near‑term goal is not perfection or full autonomy. The value today lies in time savings and scale. AI agents can handle large portions of repetitive, rules‑based work, while humans remain in the loop to review, adjust, and approve outcomes. Over time, as trust and accuracy improve, higher levels of autonomy become possible—especially for low‑risk, high‑volume categories. ⚖️

Why Data Foundations Matter More Than Models 🧠

A recurring theme in the discussion was that AI success depends less on algorithms and more on data quality and structure. Procurement data is often fragmented, inconsistently labeled, or repurposed in ways AI cannot easily interpret. AI cannot “clean” data without understanding its meaning.

This is where SAP’s investment in knowledge graphs becomes critical. By layering semantic meaning over enterprise data—defining what each field, object, and API represents in natural language—AI systems can reason about business data the same way people do. Knowledge graphs provide the connective tissue that allows AI to scale reliably across procurement, finance, and supply chain domains. 📊

Training AI Responsibly—Without Compromising Trust 🔐

One of the most common concerns from customers is how enterprise data is used by AI. Ryan clarified that SAP’s approach centers on pre‑trained models that leverage customer data at inference time, not to train shared public models.

Looking ahead, however, the greatest value will come from customer‑specific AI—models fine‑tuned to an organization’s processes, terminology, and data. This mirrors how employees are trained internally and enables AI to function as a true digital colleague. Strong governance, security, and transparency will remain essential as this capability evolves. 🧩

A New Interaction Model for Enterprise Work 👓

As AI matures, the way users interact with enterprise systems will change. Static dashboards may give way to dynamically generated views. Mobile and tablet‑based approvals will become more common. Over time, augmented and mixed‑reality interfaces may further reduce friction by delivering insights directly in context.

The unifying theme is abstraction: AI handles orchestration, data retrieval, and execution, while users focus on judgment, strategy, and outcomes. This shift has the potential to significantly reshape how procurement teams operate at scale. 🌐

The Future of Procurement Is Human‑Led, AI‑Powered 🎯

AI will not replace procurement professionals—but it will redefine their role. The future belongs to organizations that invest early in data foundations, AI literacy, and trust‑based adoption. As AI evolves from tool, to copilot, to agent, SAP’s focus remains clear: embed intelligence responsibly, keep humans in control, and deliver tangible business value.

The question is no longer whether AI will transform procurement—but how intentionally leaders choose to shape that transformation. 🔮

LISTEN HERE:

Apple:  https://podcasts.apple.com/us/podcast/episode-029-diving-deep-into-ai-in-procurement-with/id1764078646?i=1000760634770

Spotify:  Spotify:  https://open.spotify.com/episode/0DoASvDgt3tzox6D6KAmu6?si=QXxta4KzS6mO7YF2SS9MVQ

entry.title: AI authorship accusations - how do we do it exactly?
entry.content:
Hi All -

I am trying to publish a blog post about my new development for tracking operations with NFC checkpoints and its integration I did with SAP. However, my post is getting rejected because, I am citing:

This blog post is AI-generated. Purely AI-generated content is not the kind of content we are looking for in the SAP Community. Per our rules of engagement: "authors should share their persona l knowledge.

I am a bit baffled as to how to deal with it... I did use LLM to correct my grammar, but both first and final drafts were created/corrected by myself. More importantly, the development I am talking about is quite unique regardless the language. Besides, I do not see if there is a way to engage with the moderator: there is no way to reply to the feedback on "returned to author" state. Are there any criteria or tests I need to run my blog post through before submitting?

entry.title: Відкриті SAP вебінари: Manufacturing & Supply Chain
entry.content:
Квітень-травень — насичений сезон подій від SAP у сфері виробництва та Supply Chain! 🏭Реальні кейси клієнтів, живі демо та глибокі технічні інсайти — все це у трьох відкритих онлайн заходах.

Обирайте те, що актуально для вас, та реєструйтесь.

  • 21.04.2026, 15:00-16:00 (Kyiv time)
    DM, EWM Customer Talk with Centillion 
    🏢Індустрія: Life Sciences  📊 Сегмент: Medium Enterprise 🌐 Мова заходу: Англійська
    📢Спікери:
    Dimitar Lengerov -  Керівник відділу цифрових технологій
    Antoaneta Levova - Менеджер проєкту
    Kristian Markov - Керівник відділу інженерів виробничих процесів

    SAP Customer Conversations - це формат, де реальний клієнт SAP ділиться досвідом впровадження: ключовими рішеннями, викликами та досягнутими бізнес-результатами. Після презентації — LIVE дискусія та інтерактивна сесія Q&A.
    Centillion Ltd. — інноваційний болгарський виробник електронних пристроїв та систем для медицини, авіоніки та промислового обладнання. Компанія працює на сучасному екологічному виробництві площею 7 800 м².

    На заході команда поділиться досвідом впровадження RISE with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, SAP Extended Warehouse Management та SAP Digital Manufacturing — як ці рішення допомогли модернізувати бізнес-процеси, забезпечити аналітику у реальному часі, контроль якості та наскрізну цифровізацію виробництва сьогодні і в перспективі.

    Зареєструватися

  • 16.04 / 14.05 / 11.06, 20:00 – 21:00 (Kyiv time)
    Manufacturing - make to stock. MES solution
    Виробничі затримки, ручне планування та непередбачувані збої в ланцюжку постачань?

    Приєднуйтесь повторюваноно вебінару, задля живої демонстрації та дізнайтеся, як провідні виробники автоматизують процеси від планування до виконання замовлень за допомогою SAP Cloud ERP та MES.
    На вас чекає: Автоматизація MRP та виробничих замовлень, динамічне планування та оптимізація операцій за допомогою AI.

    Кількість місць обмежена.
    Зареєструватися

  • 28.04, 30.04, 05.05, 07.05, 16:00 - 17:00 (Kyiv time)
    Digital Manufacturing webinar series

    Серія вебінарів про управління тестуванням та автоматизацію для SAP Digital Manufacturing — від основ до масштабування.
    28.04 — Огляд можливостей Test Management у SAP Cloud ALM для SAP Digital Manufacturing: прозорість, відстежуваність та якість між бізнес- і ІТ-командами. Практичні приклади від партнера Сoncircle.
    30.04 — Інтеграція SAP Cloud ALM з SAP Test Automation by Tricentis: як поєднати управління тестами з автоматизацією для наскрізного забезпечення якості.
    05.05 — Повний end-to-end сценарій автоматизації тестування від Сoncircle: управління процесами, ризиками та квартальними релізами SAP Digital Manufacturing на реальних прикладах.
    07.05 — Поглиблений рівень: масштабована архітектура тест-автоматизації, шаблони, управління тестовими даними та best practices для Tosca Cloud.

    Зареєструватися

Долучайтесь, діліться з колегами, клієнтами та партнерами — ці події відкриті для всіх!

entry.title: Human-in-the-Loop SAP Agents: Approval, Escalation, and Audit (Series 2, Part 5 of 6)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  SERIES 2: AGENTIC AI IN PRACTICE  |  PART 5 OF 6

Human-in-the-Loop SAP Agents: Approval, Escalation, and Audit

Five production patterns for keeping humans in control of agentic SAP systems — approval workflows using LangGraph interrupt(), confidence-based routing, smart escalation, timeout and fallback handling, and audit trail architecture — built on SAP migration and S/4HANA change request use cases.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  Series 2, Part 5 of 6  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: Agentic AI systems that operate autonomously in SAP landscapes must have structured human checkpoints — not as a limitation, but as the architecture that makes them trustworthy enough to deploy in production. This article covers five human-in-the-loop patterns: approval workflows using LangGraph's interrupt() mechanism, confidence-based autonomous vs. escalation routing, smart escalation to the right person by exception type, timeout and fallback handling when humans don't respond, and audit trail architecture that satisfies enterprise compliance requirements. Two SAP use cases anchor the examples: migration sign-off approval from Part 4 and S/4HANA change request approval.

Series Context

This is Part 5 of the Agentic AI in Practice series. Part 4 established the principle — agents flag, humans decide. This part implements that principle in production code across five patterns with two real SAP use cases.

The patterns here apply to any agentic SAP workload — not just migration. Once you understand how LangGraph's interrupt mechanism works with Teams notifications and audit logging, you can apply it to procurement approvals, change request reviews, exception handling in any business process.

WHY HUMAN-IN-THE-LOOP IS AN ARCHITECTURE DECISION, NOT A FEATURE 

The Case for Structured Human Checkpoints

The temptation with agentic systems is to minimize human touchpoints — the less interruption, the more automation value. This is the wrong frame for SAP contexts.

SAP systems are systems of record. Actions taken in them — purchase orders raised, materials deactivated, migration objects loaded — have financial, operational, and compliance consequences that cannot be easily reversed. An agent that acts autonomously in these systems without structured human checkpoints is not more capable. It is more dangerous.

Human-in-the-loop is not about limiting what agents can do. It is about defining precisely which decisions require human judgment and ensuring those decisions always reach the right person with the right context at the right time.

Pattern

What It Does

SAP Example

Approval workflow

Agent pauses at a defined checkpoint and waits for explicit human approval before proceeding

Migration sign-off, change request authorization

Confidence-based routing

Agent decides autonomously when confidence is HIGH, escalates when MEDIUM or LOW

Data quality resolution, mapping ambiguity

Smart escalation

Agent routes to the correct person based on exception type and severity

Critical migration errors, compliance violations

Timeout and fallback

Agent handles non-response with defined fallback behavior

Approval delays during migration windows

Audit trail

Every agent decision is logged with inputs, reasoning, and outcome for compliance

All production agentic workloads

PATTERN 1: APPROVAL WORKFLOW WITH LANGGRAPH INTERRUPT() 

  ▶  LangGraph interrupt() — Agent Pauses for Human Approval 

Pattern 1: Approval Workflow

LangGraph's interrupt() function pauses graph execution at a defined node, persists the current state to a checkpointer, and waits for human input before resuming. This is the correct mechanism for approval workflows — the agent's work is preserved, the human reviews the agent's output and decision, and execution continues from exactly where it paused.

Agent completes work → interrupt() — pause → Human reviews → Approve / Reject → Resume or terminate
# Pattern 1: Approval workflow using LangGraph interrupt()
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
from langgraph.checkpoint.memory import MemorySaver
from langgraph.types import interrupt, Command
from typing import TypedDict, Literal
 
class MigrationApprovalState(TypedDict):
    sign_off_report:   str
    sign_off_status:   str    # APPROVED | CONDITIONAL | REJECTED
    discrepancies:     list
    human_decision:    str    # 'approve' | 'reject' | 'conditional'
    human_comments:    str
    final_status:      str
    audit_log:         list
 
# Node: Agent prepares sign-off package for human review
def prepare_sign_off(state: MigrationApprovalState) -> MigrationApprovalState:
    # Agent has already generated sign-off report (from Part 4 Agent 5)
    # Log the preparation step
    state['audit_log'].append({
        'timestamp': datetime.now().isoformat(),
        'action': 'SIGN_OFF_PREPARED',
        'agent_recommendation': state['sign_off_status'],
        'discrepancy_count': len(state['discrepancies'])
    })
    return state
 
# Node: interrupt() pauses execution and waits for human decision
def request_human_approval(state: MigrationApprovalState) -> MigrationApprovalState:
    """
    interrupt() suspends the graph here.
    State is persisted to checkpointer.
    Execution resumes when resume() is called with human decision.
    """
    human_input = interrupt({
        'message': 'Migration sign-off requires your approval.',
        'agent_recommendation': state['sign_off_status'],
        'report_summary': state['sign_off_report'][:500],
        'discrepancies': state['discrepancies'],
        'options': ['approve', 'conditional', 'reject']
    })
    state['human_decision'] = human_input['decision']
    state['human_comments'] = human_input.get('comments', '')
    state['audit_log'].append({
        'timestamp': datetime.now().isoformat(),
        'action': 'HUMAN_DECISION',
        'decision': human_input['decision'],
        'reviewer': human_input.get('reviewer', 'unknown'),
        'comments': human_input.get('comments', '')
    })
    return state
 
# Node: process outcome based on human decision
def process_approval_decision(state: MigrationApprovalState) -> MigrationApprovalState:
    decision = state['human_decision']
    if decision == 'approve':
        state['final_status'] = 'MIGRATION_APPROVED'
    elif decision == 'conditional':
        state['final_status'] = 'MIGRATION_APPROVED_WITH_CONDITIONS'
    else:
        state['final_status'] = 'MIGRATION_REJECTED'
    state['audit_log'].append({
        'timestamp': datetime.now().isoformat(),
        'action': 'FINAL_STATUS_SET',
        'status': state['final_status']
    })
    return state
 
# Build graph with MemorySaver checkpointer — required for interrupt()
checkpointer = MemorySaver()
approval_graph = StateGraph(MigrationApprovalState)
approval_graph.add_node('prepare', prepare_sign_off)
approval_graph.add_node('request_approval', request_human_approval)
approval_graph.add_node('process_decision', process_approval_decision)
approval_graph.add_edge('prepare', 'request_approval')
approval_graph.add_edge('request_approval', 'process_decision')
approval_graph.add_edge('process_decision', END)
approval_graph.set_entry_point('prepare')
approval_agent = approval_graph.compile(checkpointer=checkpointer, interrupt_before=['request_approval'])
 
# Resume after human provides decision
def resume_with_human_decision(thread_id, decision, reviewer, comments=''):
    config = {'configurable': {'thread_id': thread_id}}
    approval_agent.invoke(
        Command(resume={'decision': decision, 'reviewer': reviewer, 'comments': comments}),
        config=confi

A checkpointer is mandatory for interrupt() to work. Without it, graph state is not persisted and the interrupt cannot be resumed. MemorySaver works for development and testing. For production, use a database-backed checkpointer (PostgreSQL or HANA Cloud) so approval state survives application restarts.

PATTERN 2: CONFIDENCE-BASED ROUTING 

  ▶  Agent decides autonomously when confident — escalates when not 

Pattern 2: Confidence-Based Autonomous vs. Escalation Routing

Not every agent decision requires human review. Confidence-based routing lets the agent act autonomously on HIGH confidence decisions while escalating MEDIUM and LOW confidence cases. This maximizes automation value while preserving human oversight where it actually matters.

Agent decision → Assess confidence → HIGH → autonomous → MEDIUM/LOW → escalate
# Pattern 2: Confidence-based routing for S/4HANA change request
class ChangeRequestState(TypedDict):
    change_request:    dict
    risk_assessment:   dict
    confidence:        str    # 'HIGH' | 'MEDIUM' | 'LOW'
    auto_approved:     bool
    escalation_target: str
    decision:          str
    audit_log:         list
 
def assess_change_risk(state: ChangeRequestState) -> ChangeRequestState:
    """Agent analyzes change request and assigns confidence level."""
    messages = [
        SystemMessage(content='''Assess this S/4HANA change request.
        Return JSON: {risk_level: LOW|MEDIUM|HIGH|CRITICAL,
        confidence: HIGH|MEDIUM|LOW,
        auto_approve_eligible: bool,
        reasoning: str,
        escalation_role: str}
        AUTO_APPROVE only if: LOW risk, HIGH confidence, no financial impact > 10k,
        no core customizing changes, standard transport route.'''),
        HumanMessage(content=f'Change request: {json.dumps(state["change_request"])}')
    ]
    result = json.loads(llm(messages).content)
    state['risk_assessment'] = result
    state['confidence'] = result['confidence']
    state['auto_approved'] = result['auto_approve_eligible'] and result['confidence'] == 'HIGH'
    state['escalation_target'] = result.get('escalation_role', 'change_manager')
    return state
 
# Conditional edge: route based on confidence and auto-approval eligibility
def route_by_confidence(state: ChangeRequestState) -> str:
    if state['auto_approved']:
        return 'auto_approve'
    elif state['confidence'] == 'MEDIUM':
        return 'request_review'    # Human review but not full approval flow
    else:
        return 'escalate'          # Full escalation to change manager

PATTERN 3: SMART ESCALATION 

  ▶  Route exceptions to the right person based on type and severity 

Pattern 3: Smart Escalation Routing

Not all exceptions should go to the same person. A financial variance in migration reconciliation goes to the Finance Lead. A technical ABAP error goes to the Basis team. A security-related change request goes to the GRC team. Smart escalation uses AI to classify exceptions and route them to the correct stakeholder — with full context, not just an alert.

# Pattern 3: Smart escalation with role-based routing
ESCALATION_MATRIX = {
    'FINANCIAL_VARIANCE':   {'role': 'finance_lead',    'sla_hours': 2},
    'TECHNICAL_ERROR':      {'role': 'basis_team',      'sla_hours': 4},
    'DATA_QUALITY':         {'role': 'data_owner',      'sla_hours': 8},
    'SECURITY_VIOLATION':   {'role': 'grc_team',        'sla_hours': 1},
    'COMPLIANCE_RISK':      {'role': 'compliance_lead', 'sla_hours': 2},
    'PERFORMANCE_ISSUE':    {'role': 'basis_team',      'sla_hours': 4},
}
 
def smart_escalate(state, exception_text: str, context: dict) -> dict:
    """Classify exception and route to correct stakeholder via Teams + Email."""
    # Step 1: Classify exception type
    messages = [
        SystemMessage(content=f'''Classify this SAP exception.
        Return JSON: {{exception_type, severity, affected_area, recommended_action}}
        Exception types: {list(ESCALATION_MATRIX.keys())}'''),
        HumanMessage(content=f'Exception: {exception_text}\nContext: {json.dumps(context)}')
    ]
    classification = json.loads(llm(messages).content)
    exception_type = classification['exception_type']
 
    # Step 2: Look up escalation target and SLA
    escalation = ESCALATION_MATRIX.get(exception_type,
                 {'role': 'change_manager', 'sla_hours': 4})
 
    # Step 3: Generate context-rich escalation message via Claude
    msg_response = claude_client.messages.create(
        model='claude-sonnet-4-20250514', max_tokens=400,
        messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': f'''
Write a concise escalation notification for {escalation['role']}.
Exception: {exception_text}
Classification: {json.dumps(classification)}
SLA: {escalation['sla_hours']} hours. Include: what happened,
impact, recommended action, urgency. Professional tone.'''}]
    )
    escalation_message = msg_response.content[0].text
 
    # Step 4: Deliver via Teams and email
    recipient = get_role_contact(escalation['role'])
    send_teams_message(ms_token, recipient['email'], escalation_message)
    send_email(ms_token, recipient['email'],
               f"SAP Exception Escalation: {exception_type}", escalation_message)
 
    return {'type': exception_type, 'routed_to': escalation['role'],
            'sla_hours': escalation['sla_hours'], 'message': escalation_message}

PATTERN 4: TIMEOUT AND FALLBACK HANDLING 

  ▶  What happens when humans don't respond 

Pattern 4: Timeout and Fallback Handling

Migration windows are time-constrained. If an agent requests human approval at 2am and the approver doesn't respond within the SLA window, the agent needs a defined fallback behavior — not a silent hang. Three fallback strategies cover most SAP scenarios:

  • Escalate up — notify the next level in the approval chain
  • Safe halt — pause the migration, preserve state, alert all stakeholders
  • Proceed with logging — continue autonomously for LOW risk decisions, log prominently
# Pattern 4: Timeout handling with fallback strategies
import asyncio
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
 
async def request_approval_with_timeout(
    approval_request: dict,
    timeout_hours: float = 2.0,
    fallback_strategy: str = 'escalate_up'  # 'escalate_up'|'safe_halt'|'proceed'
) -> dict:
    """
    Request human approval with configurable timeout and fallback.
    Sends initial notification then polls for response.
    """
    deadline = datetime.now() + timedelta(hours=timeout_hours)
    thread_id = approval_request['thread_id']
 
    # Send initial approval request
    await send_approval_notification(approval_request)
 
    # Poll for response until deadline
    while datetime.now() < deadline:
        response = check_approval_response(thread_id)
        if response:
            return {'status': 'RESPONDED', 'decision': response}
 
        # Send reminder at 50% of timeout window
        elapsed = (datetime.now() - (deadline - timedelta(hours=timeout_hours))).total_seconds()
        if elapsed > timeout_hours * 3600 * 0.5 and not approval_request.get('reminder_sent'):
            await send_reminder_notification(approval_request)
            approval_request['reminder_sent'] = True
 
        await asyncio.sleep(300)  # Poll every 5 minutes
 
    # Timeout reached — apply fallback strategy
    return await apply_fallback(approval_request, fallback_strategy)
 
async def apply_fallback(request: dict, strategy: str) -> dict:
    if strategy == 'escalate_up':
        # Notify next level in approval chain
        escalation_target = get_next_approver(request['requested_from'])
        await send_escalation_notification(request, escalation_target)
        return {'status': 'ESCALATED', 'escalated_to': escalation_target}
 
    elif strategy == 'safe_halt':
        # Halt migration, preserve state, alert all stakeholders
        await notify_all_stakeholders(request, 'MIGRATION_HALTED_APPROVAL_TIMEOUT')
        return {'status': 'HALTED', 'reason': 'APPROVAL_TIMEOUT'}
 
    elif strategy == 'proceed':
        # Proceed autonomously — only for explicitly LOW risk decisions
        log_autonomous_proceed(request)
        return {'status': 'PROCEEDED_AUTONOMOUSLY', 'risk_accepted': True}

The 'proceed' fallback strategy should only be configured for explicitly LOW risk, fully reversible decisions. Never use 'proceed' as a fallback for migration execution, financial postings, or any action that cannot be undone. Document every instance of autonomous proceed in the audit trail with the risk acceptance rationale.

PATTERN 5: AUDIT TRAIL ARCHITECTURE 

  ▶  Every agent decision logged for compliance 

Pattern 5: Audit Trail Architecture

Enterprise compliance requirements for agentic SAP systems are not the same as application logging. An audit trail must capture not just what happened but why — what the agent's inputs were, what reasoning it applied, what it decided, and what the human review outcome was. This is what auditors and compliance teams need to validate that AI-assisted SAP decisions were appropriate.

# Pattern 5: Comprehensive audit trail for agentic SAP decisions
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict
from datetime import datetime
import hdbcli.dbapi as hdb
 
@dataclass
class AgentAuditEntry:
    entry_id:          str
    timestamp:         str
    session_id:        str    # Links all entries for one agent run
    agent_name:        str
    node_name:         str    # Which LangGraph node
    action_type:       str    # AUTONOMOUS | ESCALATED | APPROVED | REJECTED | TIMEOUT
    input_summary:     str    # Truncated inputs (no sensitive data)
    ai_reasoning:      str    # Model's reasoning for the decision
    ai_confidence:     str    # HIGH | MEDIUM | LOW
    decision:          str    # What was decided
    human_reviewer:    str    # Email if human was involved
    human_decision:    str    # Human's decision if applicable
    human_comments:    str
    sap_objects:       str    # Affected SAP objects (JSON)
    compliance_flags:  str    # Any compliance considerations flagged
 
class AgentAuditLogger:
    def __init__(self, hana_conn):
        self.conn = hana_conn
        self._ensure_audit_table()
 
    def _ensure_audit_table(self):
        self.conn.cursor().execute('''
            CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS AI_AUDIT.AGENT_DECISIONS (
                ENTRY_ID        NVARCHAR(100) PRIMARY KEY,
                TIMESTAMP       TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
                SESSION_ID      NVARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
                AGENT_NAME      NVARCHAR(100),
                NODE_NAME       NVARCHAR(100),
                ACTION_TYPE     NVARCHAR(50),
                INPUT_SUMMARY   NCLOB,
                AI_REASONING    NCLOB,
                AI_CONFIDENCE   NVARCHAR(20),
                DECISION        NCLOB,
                HUMAN_REVIEWER  NVARCHAR(200),
                HUMAN_DECISION  NVARCHAR(100),
                HUMAN_COMMENTS  NCLOB,
                SAP_OBJECTS     NCLOB,
                COMPLIANCE_FLAGS NCLOB
            )
        ''')
        self.conn.commit()
 
    def log(self, entry: AgentAuditEntry):
        cursor = self.conn.cursor()
        cursor.execute('''
            INSERT INTO AI_AUDIT.AGENT_DECISIONS VALUES
            (?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?)
        ''', list(asdict(entry).values()))
        self.conn.commit()
 
    def get_session_trail(self, session_id: str) -> list:
        """Retrieve complete audit trail for one agent run — for compliance review."""
        cursor = self.conn.cursor()
        cursor.execute('''
            SELECT * FROM AI_AUDIT.AGENT_DECISIONS
            WHERE SESSION_ID = ?
            ORDER BY TIMESTAMP ASC
        ''', [session_id])
        return cursor.fetchall()

Store audit entries in HANA Cloud — the same instance used for the vector store in Part 4. This keeps all AI-related data in one governed location with the same access controls and backup policies as your SAP operational data. Audit entries should be treated as immutable — no updates, no deletes. Append only.

MICROSOFT TEAMS INTEGRATION FOR APPROVAL NOTIFICATIONS 

Delivering Approval Requests via Microsoft Teams

The interrupt() pattern pauses the agent, but humans still need to be notified and need a way to respond. Microsoft Graph API delivers the approval request as a Teams message and captures the response. This connects the LangGraph approval flow to the communication channel SAP practitioners already use.

# Teams notification for approval request
def send_approval_request_teams(ms_token: str, approver_email: str,
                                approval_data: dict, thread_id: str):
    """
    Send structured approval request to approver via Teams.
    Includes agent recommendation, key data, and response instructions.
    """
    message_body = f'''
SAP Migration Sign-Off Request
================================
Agent Recommendation: {approval_data['agent_recommendation']}
Discrepancies Found: {approval_data['discrepancy_count']}
Migration Objects: {', '.join(approval_data['migration_objects'])}
 
Summary:
{approval_data['report_summary']}
 
To respond, reply to this message with:
APPROVE - to approve migration sign-off
CONDITIONAL: [your conditions] - to approve with conditions
REJECT: [reason] - to reject
 
Thread ID: {thread_id} (required in your response)
Response required within 2 hours.
    '''
    send_teams_message(ms_token, approver_email, message_body,
                       subject='Action Required: SAP Migration Sign-Off')

COMPLETE HUMAN-IN-THE-LOOP FLOW 

S/4HANA Change Request: Complete Human-in-the-Loop Flow

This example combines all five patterns — confidence routing, smart escalation, approval workflow, timeout handling, and audit trail — into a single change request processing flow:

CR received → Assess risk + confidence → HIGH: auto-approve → LOW/MEDIUM: request approval → Timeout? Escalate up → Audit all steps
# Complete HITL flow for S/4HANA change request processing
async def process_change_request_with_hitl(change_request: dict) -> dict:
    session_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
    audit_logger = AgentAuditLogger(hana_conn)
 
    # Step 1: Assess risk and confidence
    assessment = assess_change_risk({'change_request': change_request, ...})
    audit_logger.log(AgentAuditEntry(
        entry_id=str(uuid.uuid4()), session_id=session_id,
        agent_name='ChangeRequestAgent', node_name='assess_risk',
        action_type='AUTONOMOUS',
        ai_reasoning=assessment['risk_assessment']['reasoning'],
        ai_confidence=assessment['confidence'],
        decision=f"Risk: {assessment['risk_assessment']['risk_level']}",
        ...
    ))
 
    # Step 2: Route based on confidence
    if assessment['auto_approved']:
        audit_logger.log(AgentAuditEntry(action_type='AUTONOMOUS', ...))
        return {'status': 'AUTO_APPROVED', 'session_id': session_id}
 
    # Step 3: Request human approval with timeout
    approval_result = await request_approval_with_timeout(
        approval_request={
            'thread_id': session_id,
            'requested_from': assessment['escalation_target'],
            'change_request': change_request,
            'risk_assessment': assessment['risk_assessment']
        },
        timeout_hours=2.0,
        fallback_strategy='escalate_up'
    )
 
    # Step 4: Log final outcome
    audit_logger.log(AgentAuditEntry(
        action_type=approval_result['status'],
        human_reviewer=approval_result.get('reviewer', ''),
        human_decision=approval_result.get('decision', ''),
        ...
    ))
 
    return {'status': approval_result['status'], 'session_id': session_id}

PRODUCTION DESIGN PRINCIPLES 

Human-in-the-Loop Design Principles for SAP Agents

Principle

Implementation Guidance

Define human checkpoints before writing code

Identify which decisions require human oversight before building the agent. Adding checkpoints retroactively is significantly harder than designing them in from the start.

Use database checkpointers in production

MemorySaver loses state on restart. Production approval workflows must survive application restarts — use PostgreSQL or HANA Cloud-backed checkpointers.

Never use 'proceed' fallback for irreversible actions

Autonomous proceed is only acceptable for LOW risk, fully reversible decisions. Document every instance explicitly in the audit trail with risk acceptance rationale.

Audit trail is not optional

Every agentic decision in a production SAP system needs an auditable record. Compliance teams will ask for it. Build it from day one, not as an afterthought.

SLA-driven timeout escalation

Every approval request needs a timeout and fallback. Define SLAs by exception type — financial variances get 2 hours, data quality issues get 8. Match the urgency to the business impact.

What's Next in This Series

  • Part 6: Production Agentic SAP Systems — monitoring, observability, cost management, failure handling, and scaling agentic workloads at enterprise SAP scale

The patterns in this article — interrupt(), confidence routing, smart escalation, timeout handling, and audit trail — are the production readiness layer that separates agentic SAP proofs of concept from systems organizations can actually trust and deploy. If you implement any of these patterns and find edge cases worth documenting, share them in the comments.

entry.title: Super Admin Access
entry.content:
In our environment, Super Admin access is currently only assigned to background technical users (e.g. SFADMIN, IPSADMIN). For our transactional (IT) administrators, we use a dedicated permission group to provide access to manage and configure the system, including Manage Provisioning Access and the ability to grant Learning Administration access. This level of access is restricted to a small number of IT administrators.

With the latest release, the Manage Super Admin Access functionality has changed. As a result, our IT administrators are now classified as Admin Overseers, while our background technical users remain Super Admins. This change has prompted us to reassess our approach and raises the following questions:

  • Should background technical users be set-up as Super Admins?
  • Should our IT administrators be defined as Super Admins rather than Admin Overseers?
  • What is the rationale for including Provisioning and Learning Administration access as part of Super Admin access, given that additional permissions are still required to manage most functional areas of the system?

We’d be interested to understand how others are interpreting this change and what best practice looks like following the enhancement.

entry.title: Enhance Your AI Knowledge in SAP Finance: New Learning Journey Now Available
entry.content:
The future of finance is here, and it's powered by artificial intelligence. Are you ready to lead the charge in AI-driven financial transformation? We're excited to announce the launch of our latest learning journey: Applying Business AI Solutions and Expertise in SAP Finance

Why This Learning Journey Matters

In today's rapidly evolving business landscape, finance professionals who can harness the power of AI aren't just staying competitive; they're becoming indispensable. This intermediate learning journey is designed specifically for the next generation of finance professionals who are ready to take the lead in digital transformation and unlock the full potential of SAP's cutting-edge AI solutions.

What You'll Learn

This learning journey takes you on an immersive exploration of key AI tools that are revolutionizing finance operations:

  • SAP Joule: Discover how this intelligent assistant transforms everyday finance tasks
  • SAP Joule Agents: Learn to leverage specialized AI agents for complex financial processes
  • SAP Joule for Consultants: Master the tools that empower finance consulting excellence
  • SAP Business Data Cloud in Finance: Unlock the potential of intelligent data management

A Hands-On Learning Experience

What sets this learning journey apart is its practical, experience-driven approach. You explore Joule features in Finance firsthand through practical, hands-on exercises that enable learners to experience these capabilities themselves. You'll follow a fictitious consultant's journey as they implement AI tools across various finance business processes, giving you real-world context and actionable insights you can apply immediately.

Starting Your AI Journey? We've Got You Covered

If you're new to AI in finance, we recommend starting with our beginner-level learning journey: Discovering SAP Business AI Capabilities for SAP Finance. This foundational course introduces you to the core concepts and capabilities of AI in the finance domain, providing the perfect stepping stone before diving into the more advanced, hands-on content in our new intermediate learning journey. Whether you're taking your first steps or ready to deepen your expertise, we have a path designed for your learning needs.

Curious?

The intersection of AI and finance represents one of the most exciting opportunities in today's business world. By completing our learning journey, you'll not only gain technical expertise but also position yourself as an expert in the AI-driven future of finance. Start learning right now!

entry.title: SAP Success Pläne - Der Guide zum neuen SAP Support und Service Modell 2026
entry.content:
Dieser Blog ist eine Zusammenfassung verschiedener Quellen zu den Success Plänen von SAP. Stand: April 2026. Zudem verlinke ich zunehmend Details wie vertiefende Informationen zu den Success Plänen.

Inhaltsverzeichnis

  1. Das Wichtigste in Kürze
  2. Warum SAP sein Support-Modell umstellt
  3. Die drei SAP Success Pläne im Überblick
    3.1 Foundational Success Plan 
    3.2 Advanced Success Plan
    3.3 Max Success Plan
  1. Vergleich: Foundational vs. Advanced vs. Max
  2. Was sich gegenüber dem alten Modell ändert
  3. Continuous Transformation und Business AI
  4. Entscheidungshilfe: Welcher Plan passt?
  5. Migration  
  6. Häufige Fragen (FAQ)
  7. Weiterführende Artikel und Deep Dives
  8. Fazit

1. SAP Support in der Cloud - Die Success Pläne von SAP 

SAP hat am 3. Maerz 2026 ein neues Service- und Supportportfolio für Cloud-Kunden eingeführt. Das bisherige Nebeneinander aus Enterprise Support, Preferred Success und Premium Engagement weicht einem klar gestaffelten Modell aus drei aufeinander aufbauenden Success Plänen:

  • Foundational Success Plan - automatisch in jeder SAP-Cloud-Lösung enthalten. Deckt Onboarding, technischen Cloud-Betrieb, präventiven 24/7-Support und Self-Service-Ressourcen ab.
  • Advanced Success Plan - ergänzt spezialisierte Expertise, KI-gestützte Guidance, proaktive Risikoerkennung, verbesserte SLAs und einen Success Plan Advisor.
  • Max Success Plan - die strategische Partnerschaft. Enthält alle Advanced-Leistungen plus dedizierte Success Plan Manager, lösungsübergreifende Prozessoptimierung und kundenspezifische KI-Prototypenentwicklung.

Das Modell gilt ausschließlich für SAP-Cloud-Lösungen. On-Premise-Kunden behalten ihren bestehenden Support. Bestehende Verträge werden zum Laufzeitende oder auf Wunsch früher überführt.

2. Warum SAP sein Support und Service Modell umstellt

Enterprise Support, Preferred Success, Expanded Edition, ActiveAttention, MaxAttention, Premium Engagement Services - die Bezeichnungen waren vielfältig, die Abgrenzungen nicht immer ganz trennscharf. Für Kunden sollte es einfacher sein in Zukunft, das passende Support-Paket zu identifizieren.

Gleichzeitig erfordern Cloud-Lösungen einen anderen Service & Support: automatisiertes Monitoring, KI-gestützte Empfehlungen und kontinuierliche Innovation statt Fokus auf einmaliger Implementierung. SAP reagierte daher mit einer Vereinfachung.

With our renewed SAP Services and Support portfolio, we provide [customers] a clearer path to adopt innovation, apply AI responsibly, and run their landscape with confidence. - SAP Executive Board Member Thomas Saueressig (SAPNews)

 Die zentralen Treiber:

  • Vereinfachung: Drei klare Stufen statt ein Flickenteppich aus Einzelangeboten
  • Cloud-Fokus: Support für automatisiertes Monitoring und kontinuierliche Updates
  • KI-Integration: Joule und intelligentes Monitoring als fester Bestandteil
  • Transparenz: Klar definierte Leistungsstufen mit nachvollziehbaren SLAs
  • Kontinuierliche Transformation: Vom einmaligen Projekt zur fortlaufenden Partnerschaft

3. Die drei Success Pläne von SAP im Überblick

Die drei Pläne bauen aufeinander auf. Kunden können für unterschiedliche SAP-Lösungen unterschiedliche Pläne wählen.

“Like a car, I can strategically steer the engagement in the direction that will serve our needs.”
- Vinci Energies CIO Dominique Tessaro

3.1 Foundational Success Plan - Die Basis für alle Cloud-Kunden

In jeder SAP-Cloud-Lösung ist dieser Plan ohne zusätzliche Kosten enthalten. Er baut auf Enterprise Support auf und erweitert ihn um Cloud-spezifische Komponenten. Er ist für Unternehmen geeignet mit erfahrenen internen Teams, die ihre SAP-Umgebung weitgehend eigenständig betreiben.

Enthaltene Leistungen:

  • Präventiver 24/7-Support für geschäftskritische Systeme mit definierten SLAs
  • SAP Cloud ALM: Guidance und Monitoring für den gesamten Anwendungslebenszyklus
  • Self-Service-Ressourcen: Lösungsdokumentation, kuratierte Inhalte, Learning Journeys
  • Joule: KI-Assistent fuer Echtzeit-Unterstützung
  • SAP for Me: Zentrales Kundenportal fuer Support-Cases und Systemstatus

>> Deep Dive: Foundational Success Plan im Detail (folgt)

3.2 Advanced Success Plan - Proaktive Guidance 

Dieser Plan löst SAP Preferred Success und SAP Preferred Success Expanded Edition ab. Er eignet sich für Unternehmen mit komplexeren Landschaften und höherem Innovationsbedarf.

Zusätzlich zu allen Foundational-Leistungen:

  • Verbesserte SLAs: Beschleunigte Reaktionszeiten
  • KI-gestütztes Monitoring: Intelligente Qualität- und Performance-Überwachung
  • Proaktive Risikoerkennung  
  • Aktivierungssessions für neue Funktionalitäten und Releases
  • Gezielte Product Release Guidance
  • Success Plan Advisor als persönliche Ansprechpartner
  • Business AI Guidance: Potenzialanalyse, Aktivierung, Optimierungsempfehlungen

>> Deep Dive: Advanced Success Plan im Detail (folgt)

3.3 Max Success Plan - die Strategische Partnerschaft

Dies ist SAPs umfassendstes Engagement-Angebot und integriert die bisherigen Premium Engagement Services. Für komplexe Transformationsprojekte und eine enge strategische Partnerschaft mit SAP.

Zusätzlich zu allen Advanced-Leistungen:

  • Dedizierte Success Plan Manager fuer ganzheitliche Betreuung
  • Lösungsübergreifende End-to-End-Prozessoptimierung
  • Kundenspezifische KI-Prototypenentwicklung
  • Strategische Roadmap: KI-Strategie, Daten- und Architekturplanung
  • Vor-Ort-Support bei kritischen Projekten
  • Viele weitere inkludierte Services, z.B. rund um die integrierte SAP Toolchain

>> Deep Dive: Max Success Plan im Detail (folgt)

4. Vergleich: Foundational vs. Advanced vs. Max

Hier ist eine Gegeüberstellung der drei Varianten:

5. Was sich gegenüber dem alten Modell ändert

Die Umstellung betrifft ausschließlich SAP Cloud-Kunden.

Vorher: Ein fragmentiertes Portfolio

  • Enterprise Support: Basis für alle Kunden
  • Preferred Success / Preferred Success Expanded Edition: Erweiterter Cloud-Support als Abonnement
  • ActiveAttention / MaxAttention / Premium Engagement: verschiedene Premium-Angebote

Nachher: Drei klare Stufen

  • Foundational = erweitert Enterprise Support (Cloud)
  • Advanced = loest Preferred Success ab
  • Max = integriert Premium Engagement Services

6. Kontinutierliche Transformation und Business AI

Hinter den Success Plänen steht ein Paradigmenwechsel: weg von punktuellen einzelnen Projekten, hin zu kontinuierlicher Transformation. Support greift nicht erst bei Problemen, sondern agiert proaktiv, durchgängig und KI-gestützt.

  • Durchgängig: Begleitung des gesamten Kundenlebenszyklus
  • Proaktiv statt reaktiv: KI-Monitoring erkennt Risiken vor Geschäftsauswirkungen
  • Skalierbar: Pro Lösung kann man den passenden Plan wählen und bei Bedarf wechseln

Die Futurum Group kommentierte: "SAP hat sein Portfolio erneuert, um Unternehmen den Übergang von episodischen Transformationsprojekten zu einem Modell des kontinuierlichen, messbaren Fortschritts zu ermöglichen."

7. Entscheidungshilfe: Welcher Plan passt für mich?

Die Wahl hängt von Komplexität, internen Kapazitäten und Innovationsbedarf ab.

Foundational:

  • Erfahrenes internes SAP-Team vorhanden
  • Sehr standardnahe Cloud-Landschaft
  • Self-Service-Ressourcen ausreichend

Advanced:

  • Komplexere Landschaft mit mehreren Cloud-Lösungen
  • Proaktive Release- und Innovationsbegleitung nötig
  • Kürzere, bessere SLAs und KI-Potenzialanalyse gewünscht

Max:

  • Grosse, heterogene, hybride Landschaften mit lösungsübergreifenden Abhängigkeiten
  • Komplexe Transformation oder strategische KI-Partnerschaft
  • Dedizierter Manager gewünscht

Tipp: Für unterschiedliche Lösungen koennen verschiedene Pläne kombiniert werden - z. B. Advanced für SuccessFactors, Max für SAP Cloud ERP.

8. Migration und Preise

Übergang zum neuen Modell

  • Neue Cloud-Verträge ab 3. Maerz 2026 direkt im neuen Modell
  • Bestehende Verträge: Überführung zum Laufzeitende oder auf Wunsch früher
  • Entscheidung ist lösungsbezogen - nicht pauschal für die gesamte Landschaft

Nächste Schritte für bestehende SAP Kunden:

  1. Bestandsaufnahme: Welche Cloud-Lösungen und welchen Support-Vertrag haben Sie?
  2. Leistungsvergleich: Deckt Foundational Ihren Bedarf, oder brauchen Sie mehr?
  3. Budgetplanung: Advanced und Max sind kostenpflichtig.
  4. Kontakt: Sprechen Sie mit Ihrem SAP Account Executive oder Services Kontakt.

 9. Häufige Fragen (FAQ)

Was sind die SAP Success Pläne?

Das neue, dreistufige Service- und Supportmodell von SAP für Cloud-Kunden: Foundational (in der Cloud-Lösung enthalten), Advanced (erweiterter Support mit KI-Guidance) und Max (strategische Partnerschaft mit dedizierten Experten). Eingeführt am 3. Maerz 2026.

Wann treten die Success Pläne in Kraft?

Seit dem 3. Maerz 2026. Neue Cloud-Verträge werden im neuen Modell abgeschlossen. Bestehende Verträge werden zum Laufzeitende oder auf Wunsch vorzeitig ueberführt.

Was kostet Advanced bzw. Max?

Vertragsabhängig - SAP veröffentlicht keine Listenpreise. Konkrete Preise über Ihren SAP Account Executive.

Muss ich als Cloud-Kunde etwas tun?

Kurzfristig nein - Foundational ist automatisch enthalten. Prüfen Sie frühzeitig, ob Advanced oder Max zu Ihrem Bedarf passt.

Gilt das Modell für On-Premise?

Nein. Die Success Pläne gelten ausschließlich für SAP-Cloud-Lösungen.

Kann ich verschiedene Pläne pro Lösung wählen?

Ja. Volle Flexibilitaet - z. B. Advanced fuer SuccessFactors, Max fuer die KI-Strategie oder Cloud ERP.

Was passiert mit Preferred Success?

Wird durch den Advanced Success Plan abgelöst. Migration zum Vertragslaufzeitende oder auf Wunsch früher.

Advisor vs. Manager - was ist der Unterschied?

Der Advisor (Advanced) ist persönlicher Ansprechpartner für die strategische Ausrichtung. Der Success Plan Manager (Max) ist dediziert, kennt Ihre Landschaft im Detail und beratet Sie lösungsübergreifend.

Welche Rolle spielt Cloud ALM?

Teil des Foundational Plans für alle SAP Cloud-Kunden: für Monitoring, Change Management, Operations. Im Advanced um KI-Monitoring erweitert.

Wie helfen die Pläne bei KI Einführungen?

Foundational: Zugriff auf Lösungsdokumentation über help.sap.com, Learning Journeys, Aktivierung von Joule über Self-Services. 
Advanced: Zusätzliche Leistungen zur Identifikation des Business AI Potenzials, strategische Aktivierung von Anwendungsfällen, fortlaufende Produktberatung, Guidance und Optimierungsempfehlungen. 
Max: Definition der KI-Strategie, Erstellung einer Daten- und Architektur-Roadmap, Entwicklung einer Strategie und von Prototypen für maßgeschneiderte KI-Anwendungsfälle.

10. Weiterführende Artikel und Deep Dives

Weitere Infos werden hier im Folgenden 

  • Foundational Success Plan im Detail
  • Advanced Success Plan im Detail
  • Max Success Plan im Detail

11. Fazit

Die SAP Success Pläne vereinfachen in ein über Jahre gewachsenes Service und Sport Portfolio. Foundational bietet allen Cloud-Kunden eine solide Basis ohne Zusatzkosten - mit Cloud ALM, Joule und 24/7-Support. Advanced und Max liefern abgestufte Upgrades für Unternehmen mit höherem Bedarf an SLAs, proaktivem Monitoring oder strategischer Partnerschaft.

Die Frage ist, welchen Plan Ihr Unternehmen braucht, um das Maximum aus seiner SAP-Cloud-Investition herauszuholen.

Wo finden sich weiterführende Informationen? Im März folgt eine dreiteilige Webcast-Reihe in Deutsch und Englisch mit den Schwerpunkten: Überblick über die Success Pläne/ Deep Dive Advanced Success Plan / Deep Dive Max Success Plan. Die Anmeldung ist hier noch möglich.

Zusätzlich ist am 25.06. ein Vor-Ort Event geplant in Walldorf – Details folgen hier.

Quellen

SAP News (DE): SAP entwickelt Service- und Supportportfolio weiter, 3. März 2026 
SAP News: Orchestrating Continuous Transformation, Thomas Pfiester, März 2026
SAP Community: SAP Success Plans - Der neue Support-Ansatz fuer die Cloud,  05.02.2026 
CIO.com: SAP shakes up its service and support portfolio, März 2026
Computerwoche: SAP stellt Support-Portfolio um, März 2026
Futurum Group: SAP New Services Portfolio, März   2026

entry.title: Re: Material inconsistencies during WIP step of Material...
entry.content:
Hello Jessica,

The inconsistency error is extremely complex. The error can be caused from the difference between MM-ML table, or MM-FI table, or ML-FI table, or ML-ML table... Only 1 error message (ex: C+048) can be fixed by several solutions/ reports depend on your data. Updating inconsistencies by wrong report can make your data worse. Due to its danger and risky, only SAP Support team can handle to fix and correct the inconsistency.

About pre-check inconsistency before the period end closing, you can use CKMHELP to check, but cannot correct due to above reason.

But as you can see there are a lot of reports in CKMHELP, I cannot suggest which reports should be run for each period end. Because in March you may facing MM-ML inconsistency, but in April you may facing ML-FI inconsistency. So please note that this checking will take you a lot of effort'; and the error from CKMLCP not always come from the inconsistency, but maybe due to missing SAP Note. 

So I think with the inconsistency error, create an OSS, apply Note for permanent solution or Upgrade the system should be recommended.

Have a good day!
